5. GOVERNOR AUTHORIZED TO COMMISSION
NECESSARY OFFICERS :
The Governor of Kentucky is hereby authorized
to appoint and commission the necessary officers for
the Kentucky Active Militia . The Adjutant General
of the State with the approval of the Governor
shall prescribe rules and regulations governing the
enlistment , organization , administration , pay, equip-
ment , discipline and discharge of the personnel of
the Kentucky Active Militia ; obtain by requisition
or purchase from the Secretary of War such neces-
sary arms and equipment as may be secured from
the War Department , or procure from some other
source the necessary arms and equipment to main-
tain and equip the Kentucky Active Militia ; and
provide the necessary space for the housing of
property and for the purpose of drills and instruc-
tion .
6. COMMISSIONS FOR OFFICERS AND ENLISTMENTS
AND DISCHARGES FOR ENLISTED MEN :
All officers shall be appointed and commissioned
by the Governor . All men shall be enlisted by the
unit commanders and shall be discharged upon
order of the Governor for the same reasons as men
are discharged from the National Guard , when such
discharge is requested in writing by the unit com-
manders .

MEN :

quired to be taken by commissioned officers and en-

8. THE GOVERNOR MAY DISBAND AND REORGANIZE
THE KENTUCKY ACTIVE MILITIA :
The Governor shall have the power to alter ,
divide , annex , disband or reorganize any organiza-
tion of the Kentucky Active Militia whenever in his
judgment the efficiency of the State forces will
thereby be increased , and he shall have power to
change the organization so as to conform to the
regulations now or hereafter prescribed by the laws
of the United States for the organization of the
National Guard .
9. GOVERNOR AUTHORIZED TO CALL KENTUCKY
ACTIVE MILITIA INTO ACTIVE SERVICE :
The Governor is hereby authorized to call and to
assign any part of the Kentucky Active Militia to
active service for the purpose of resisting invasion ,
suppressing rebellions , insurrections , riots or
threats thereof ; to suppress any active , unlawful
or threatened violence to persons or property in
this State ; to authorize the arrest of all persons
engaged in aiding or abetting therein ; to investigate
any acts of treason , sabotage or attempted sabotage
and to cause the arrest of such persons engaged
therein for aiding and abetting ; to protect life and
property in the event of any emergency or disaster .
The Governor may by his order prescribe the duty

CHAPTER 8
(H. B. 89 )
AN ACT relating to appropriations for the Division of
Banking .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Subsection (7) of section 1 , of Chapter 30
of the 1940 Acts of the General Assembly is
amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) All moneys collected by the Division of
Banking , from any source , during any fiscal year , in
excess of the amount appropriated out of the
General Expenditure Fund to the Division of Bank-
ing for that fiscal year , shall be placed in a revolving
fund in the State Treasury to be expended for the
ordinary recurring expenses of operation of the
Division of Banking .
( 2 ) All moneys collected by the Division of
Banking during the fiscal year from July 1 , 1941
to June 30 , 1942 , in excess of the moneys appro-
priated to the Division of Banking out of the
General Expenditure Fund for that fiscal year shall
be placed in the revolving fund created by this
section .

Section III . Qualifications of applicants for
Policemen and Firemen .
The Commission shall require all applicants for
appointments as members of the police or fire de-
partments to be examined as to their qualifications
to fill the office of policeman or fireman , and as to
their knowledge of the English language , and as
to the law and rules governing the duties of police-
men and firemen . Every member of the police or
fire department shall be able to read and write and
understand the English language , and to have such
other general qualifications as may be prescribed .
No person shall be appointed a member of the
police or fire departments , unless he is well known
to be a man of sobriety and integrity , and has been
and is an orderly , law-abiding citizen , nor shall any
person be appointed as a member of said police or
fire department on account of any political , partisan
service rendered by him or on account of political
sentiments or affiliations , or who is under 21 years
of age or over forty, unless the applicant has had
as much as five years ' experience as a regular
policeman or fireman and is not over fifty five ( 55 )
years of age . No member of the police or fire de
partments shall be removed or discharged or re-
duced in grade or pay for any political partisan
opinion . The appointment and continuance upon
the police and fire departments shall depend solely
upon the ability and willingness of a person to com-
ply with the rules of the said departments and to
perform the duties of said departments . No ap-
pointment to or continuance as a member of a police

Section IV . Personnel of the Commission
The Mayor , by and with the approval of the
Legislative Body , shall appoint three citizens , who
have been taxpayers and voters of the city for five
(5 ) years previous to their appointment , and who
shall not be less than thirty ( 30 ) years of age, who
shall constitute the Civil Service Commission of said
city . Such appointees shall originally be appointed
for one year , two years , and three years , respec-
tively , and the successors of such appointees shall
be appointed in like manner , each for a period of
three years . Any member of the Civil Service Com-
mission shall be eligible for reappointment .

classification.title: An Act to amend an Act entitled "An Act to provide for the pensioning of Policemen and Firemen in cities of the first class."
classification.summary: This act allows legislative bodies of cities to appropriate funds from the General Fund for a pension fund for policemen and firemen. Eligibility for pensions is based on years of service (at least 20 years) and age (at least 51). It also covers pensions for those permanently crippled in service and for the widows or dependent children of deceased members. All rewards, fees, gifts, and emoluments for extraordinary service are to be paid into the pension fund. The Board of Trustees of the fund is empowered to invest the fund.

bond approved by the Governor , and take the oath
required by Section 228 of the Constitution . The
Secretary of State shall have custody of the bond.

endorsed upon the commission issued to the special
policeman . The person making application for ap-
pointment under the provisions of this Act shall
pay a fee of $ 1.00 only to the Secretary of State to
be placed in the Revolving Fund and used by the
Secretary of State only to defray the expenses of
administering this Act .

classification.summary: This act establishes a classified civil service system under a merit system for certain positions within cities of the first class, specifically in the Departments of Public Safety, Public Health, Public Welfare, and the Civil Service Board. It outlines procedures for extending this classified service to other city positions and those under joint city-county control. The act also details the creation, appointment, qualifications, terms, and removal of Civil Service Board members, their powers and duties, and provisions for compensation and expenses. It prohibits political activity and defines other violations as misdemeanors, with penalties prescribed. The act also authorizes necessary tax levies and appropriations by the cities and repeals inconsistent acts.

Emergency Appointment " means any appoint-
ment to any position subject to the provisions of
this act , which may be made only in the absence of
an appropriate eligible list for the position and
which may be made without competitive examina-
tion pending the establishment of an eligible list and

the emergency and in no case to exceed ninety days

provisions of Section 5 of this act and which shall
not be less than six months nor more than one year
in duration .
" Regular Appointment " means an appointment
to a position subject to the provisions of this act
made after an employee has served a probationary
period in a manner deemed satisfactory to the
appointing authority .
" Director " means the Personnel Director , as
provided for in Section 3 of this act.
"Position " means every place of employment in
the classified service hereunder including office .
" Employee " means any person in the classified
service hereunder .

No member of the said Board shall be eligible for

office , he thereby vacates his position as a member

Any member of the Board may be removed by the

after at least ten days notice in writing shall have
been given to the member , which notice shall con-
tain a copy of charges preferred against him . At
the hearing he may be represented by counsel .
The finding of the Mayor shall be final and removal
results in vacancy in such office .
Each member of the Board may be paid a salary
not to exceed a per diem allowance of $ 10.00 for
each meeting attended , provided , however , that the
annual allowance of any one member of the Board
shall not exceed the sum of $ 500.00 in any one fiscal
year , and in addition thereto members shall be
reimbursed for necessary travel and other expenses
incurred in connection with their duties as members .
The Board shall hold at least one regular meeting
in each month and shall hold such additional meet-
ings as may be necessary .

classification.title: CHAPTER 16 125
classification.summary: This statute outlines the appointment, terms, removal, and compensation of members of a city board. It specifies that initial appointments will have staggered terms of two, three, and four years, with subsequent appointments for four-year terms. Vacancies are to be filled for the unexpired portion of the term. Members are eligible for reappointment but cannot hold other appointive city offices while serving or for one year after. Candidacy for elective office vacates a board position. The Mayor can remove members for cause after a hearing and written notice. Board members can receive a per diem allowance for meetings, capped annually, and are reimbursed for expenses. The Board must hold at least one monthly meeting.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and operational aspects of a city board, including appointment procedures, term lengths, removal processes, and compensation for its members. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The language used is purely procedural and administrative, focusing on the governance of the board itself. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

Section 4. EMINENT DOMAIN PROCEED-
INGS .
Any board proceeding under this Act shall have
the right to acquire by the exercise of the power
of eminent domain , all lands , easements , rights of
way, either upon or under or above the ground ,
any existing electric plant , or that part of an elec-
tric plant within the corporate limits of such city ,
and any and all real estate , franchise or personal
property reasonably necessary or desirable in con-
nection with the construction or operation or main-
tenance of electric plants or improvements or exten-
sions thereto ; and , the right of such board to
acquire such electric plant and facilities hereby is
declared to be a superior and paramount right and
superior and paramount to any other public use .
Provided , however , that no board, by exercise of
the power of eminent domain , shall have the right
to acquire any central generating plant or station
or sub -station or transmission lines , dams , or other
property or facilities primarily and principally used

and transmission of electric energy by such public
or private utility for use outside the area to be

classification.title: An Act Relating to Electric Plant Boards
classification.summary: This act establishes the Electric Plant Board of a city as a body politic and corporate. It outlines appointment criteria for board members, prohibiting those who have held public office within the last two years or are related to the mayor or governing body within the third degree. It also prohibits the board or superintendent from appointing or employing individuals related within the third degree to board members, the mayor, or governing body members. Additionally, it states that no officer or employee of a municipality can be eligible for such appointment until at least one year after their term expires, with personal liability for violations. Board members must be citizens, taxpayers, and legal voters, not indebted to the municipality, and not sureties on official bonds.

classification.title: An Act to amend Chapter 193 of the Acts of the 1940 regular session, relating to the duties and powers of a commission and its executive director.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 4(d) of Chapter 193 of the 1940 Acts. It clarifies the duties and powers of a commission and its Executive Director. The Executive Director is empowered to appoint personnel, make expenditures, require reports, conduct investigations, and take administrative actions for the proper administration of the Chapter, in accordance with the Governmental Re-organization Act of 1936. The Commission retains the power to adopt, amend, or rescind rules and regulations, elect coverage for its workers under specific provisions or the Workmen's Compensation Law, determine its organization and procedures, and maintain an official seal. The Commission is also required to submit an annual report to the Governor by February 1st, detailing the administration and operation of the Chapter during the preceding calendar year and making recommendations for amendments.

The benefits paid to an eligible worker for a
week of unemployment shall be charged proportion-
ately against the accounts of those of his subject
employers from whom he has base year or base
period wages . The proportion so used shall be that
ratio which is obtained by dividing the base year or
base period wages of a worker from such employer
by the total base year or base period wages of such
worker , adjusted to the nearest multiple of one per
cent .
For the purposes of this paragraph a fractional
part of a cent shall be disregarded unless it amounts
to one -half cent or more in which case it shall be
increased to one cent .
Section 9. Declaration of Emergency .
Whereas , the passage of the provisions of this
Act is necessary to secure federal credit , under the
provisions of the Federal Statutes , for unemploy-
ment compensation taxes paid by Kentucky em-
ployers , for the entire year 1942 , an emergency is
hereby declared to exist . and this Act shall become
law upon its approval by the Governor .

This Act shall be construed liberally to secure the

tration .
Section 18 . Title .
This Act may be cited as the " Uniform Veterans '
Guardianship Act . "
Section 19. Effect of invalidity of Act or part
thereof .
The invalidity of any portion of this Act shall not
affect the validity of any other portion thereof
which can be given effect without such invalid part .
Section 20. Modification of Prior Laws.-
All laws or parts of laws relating to beneficiaries
of the Veterans Administration inconsistent with
this Act are hereby repealed . Except where incon-
sistent with this Act , the general guardianship laws
of this State and the laws establishing the practice in
such matters including rights of appeal , shall be
applicable to such beneficiaries and their estates .
Section 21. Application of Act.-
The provisions of this Act relating to surety
bonds and the administration of estates of Veterans
Administration beneficiaries under guardianship
shall apply to all such estates heretofore or here-
after created under the general laws of this State
to the extent that such estates have assets derived
from benefits paid by the Veterans Administration
or receive such benefits in the future .
Section 22 .
Sections 2043-1 to 2043-20 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes , 1936 edition are repealed .

It clarifies the requirements for signatures on certificates, including the use of facsimile signatures by transfer agents and registrars, and addresses situations where officers or agents who signed a certificate cease their employment before delivery.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with corporate law, specifically the issuance and transfer of stock certificates. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and reenact Section 2380c - 1 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , 1936 edition .
classification.summary: This act amends Section 2380c-1 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edition. It mandates that in counties with seventy-five or more separate drainage districts, the County court shall appoint a drainage commissioner. This appointed commissioner will possess all powers and duties of directors and Boards of Drainage Commissioners elected under Section 2380-7. The appointed commissioner will serve a four-year term.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the appointment of drainage commissioners in counties with a specific number of drainage districts. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates the subject matter and lack of racial indicators.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to and amending the charter of cities of the first class, being Chapter 89-1 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, Baldwin's 1936 Revision; amending, revising and reenacting section 2802 thereof, relating to the executive departments in such cities, and establishing an executive department of public parks and recreation; repealing and reenacting section 2840 thereof relating to public parks and the board of park commissioners in such cities; repealing and reenacting section 2852 thereof relating to condemnation for park purposes; abolishing said board of park commissioners and transferring the functions, powers, rights, duties, liabilities, properties as defined herein, including but not limited to airports and aviation fields, and personnel, of said board to such city, and assigning same to certain executive departments of said city; and defining the rights, powers and duties of said departments with reference thereto; defining the rights and powers of said city with reference to said property and exempting said property from taxation; providing for the administration and disposition of funds in the hands of board and of monies hereafter to be collected for park purposes; providing for the organization and
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of first-class cities in Kentucky, specifically concerning executive departments, public parks, and recreation. It establishes a new department of public parks and recreation, revises provisions related to public parks and the board of park commissioners, and addresses condemnation for park purposes. The act abolishes the board of park commissioners and transfers its functions, powers, and properties (including airports) to the city, assigning them to specific executive departments. It also defines the city's rights and powers regarding park property, exempts it from taxation, and outlines the administration of park funds.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative structure of cities, specifically regarding parks and recreation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is on municipal governance and the management of public spaces. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act relating to the administration of the department of public parks and recreation in cities of the first class.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 2802 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes to establish executive departments in cities of the first class. It details the administration of the department of public parks and recreation, including its rights, powers, and duties. It also addresses the transfer of special park police and recreation functions, authorizes property condemnation for park purposes, and repeals conflicting laws. The act declares an emergency.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative structure and functions of public parks and recreation departments in first-class cities. It details the transfer of personnel and powers related to park police and recreation services. While it repeals several sections of the Kentucky Statutes, none of the text explicitly or implicitly refers to race or racial segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the text is clear and does not contain any racial indicators.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the establishment and maintenance of public parks and playgrounds in first-class cities. It grants the director of the parks department the power to adopt rules and regulations for the use, management, and control of park property, and to organize the department into divisions. It also defines "park property" broadly to include various park amenities and land. The act also transfers title of park property to the city.

It focuses on the management and control of park property and the structure of the parks department. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to the Department of Public Parks and Recreation and civil service laws.
classification.summary: This act transfers employees of the department of public parks and recreation under civil service laws, with specific exceptions. It clarifies that transferred employees are considered to have satisfied qualifications and are given probationary appointments. Certain positions, including janitors, janitresses, laundresses, cleaners, and laborers, are specifically excepted from civil service provisions but can be added later by ordinance, except for the Director's office.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the application of civil service laws to employees of a public parks and recreation department. It details the process of transferring employees and lists specific positions that are excepted from these civil service regulations. While it mentions 'janitors' and 'janitresses', which could have been gendered roles, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1942.

classification.title: CHAPTER 34 233
classification.summary: This act transfers special park police in cities of the first class to the division of police of the department of public safety. It outlines their employment status, qualifications, and probationary appointments. It also grants these officers arrest powers within city parks, park property, parkways, and park boulevards, as well as in the county outside city limits if the offense occurred on park property. Arrested individuals are to be tried in the jurisdiction where the offense was committed.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative transfer and powers of special park police within a city's public safety department. It details employment conditions and arrest authority. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: CHAPTER 36 249
classification.summary: This statute declares that the clearance, replanning, rehabilitation, and reconstruction of substandard and insanitary areas in cities are necessary for public welfare. It defines such areas as those found by a planning commission to be substandard or insanitary, requiring clearance, replanning, rehabilitation, or reconstruction to effectuate public purposes. The statute outlines the necessity of acquiring adequate areas at fair prices, demolishing obsolete buildings, and redeveloping these areas under proper supervision with appropriate planning and land use policies. It asserts that these actions constitute public uses and purposes for which private property may be acquired, as such conditions menace the health, safety, morals, welfare, and comfort of citizens.

classification.title: CHAPTER 36 261
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a redevelopment corporation would lose its special rights and privileges. It specifies that if a corporation fails to obtain necessary certificates of approval for its development plan within twelve months, or fails to substantially comply with the plan within the stipulated timeframes (with exceptions for unforeseen difficulties), it must cease to operate as a redevelopment corporation. Upon such failure, the corporation must change its name to remove the word "redevelopment" and can continue as a general corporation. Any real property acquired by condemnation must be disposed of by bona fide sale, with excess proceeds paid to the city. The statute also prohibits corporations from adopting names containing the word "redevelopment" under certain circumstances.

CHAPTER 36 277
SECTION 18. CERTIFICATES OF COM-
PLETION, SUPERVISION AND CONTROL
AFTER MORTGAGE OR SALE .
(1) It shall be the duty of the supervising
agency , upon any request from a redevelopment
corporation , to determine whether the redevelop-
ment of any unit or units in the development area
has been achieved , and if the supervising agency
finds that such redevelopment has been finished and
achieved it shall issue to the redevelopment cor-
poration a certificate of completion showing that
the redevelopment of such parcel or parcels has or
have been completed , describing such unit or units .
(2 ) It shall be the duty of the supervising
agency , upon request from a redevelopment cor-
poration , to determine whether the redevelopment
of the development area has been achieved , and if
the supervising agency finds that such redevelop-
ment has been finished and achieved it shall issue
to the redevelopment corporation a certificate of
completion showing that the redevelopment of such
area has been completed . No such determination
or finding shall affect any unit or units for which
certificates of completion have been issued under
paragraph 1 of this section of this act .
(3 ) Such redevelopment property , or unit
thereof , may be sold or mortgaged ; Provided , how-
ever , that such sale or mortgage shall be authorized
as herein provided and shall , after such sale or
mortgage , be used for the general purposes of this
act and maintained in accordance with the stand-
ards provided therefor by the planning commission
of such city in the original certificate of approval
issued for such redevelopment under and pursuant
to the provisions of paragraph 3 of Section 3 of
this act .

CHAPTER 38
(S. B. 37)
AN ACT to amend and reenact Section 965-14 of Carroll's
Kentucky Statutes 1936 Edition relating to and fixing the
time for holding Court in the Fourteenth Judicial District ,
composed of the counties of Bourbon , Franklin , Scott and
Woodford .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 965-14 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes , 1936 Edition, be amended and re -enacted
so that the same when so amended and re -enacted
shall read as follows :
Fourteenth District . Franklin County , at Frank-
fort , on the first Monday in January , twenty -four
juridical days , first Monday in April , thirty juridi-
cal days , and the first Monday in September ,
twenty-four juridical days .
Scott County , at Georgetown , on the first Mon-
day in February , and October , and the fourth Mon-
day in May , eighteen juridical days each .
Woodford County , at Versailles , on the fourth
Monday in February and October and the second
Monday in May , twelve juridical days each .
Bourbon County , at Paris , on the second Mon-
day in March and November , eighteen juridical
days each , and the third Monday in June , twelve
juridical days .

312 ACTS OF THE GENERAL Assembly
WHEREAS the operation of local governments are becoming
increasingly more difficult due to the mounting cost of
supplies , and whereas more rigid economy must be effected
by the various local units of government in order that our
citizens may the better assist in our program of National
Defense , and whereas a great saving may be effected by
mass purchasing of supplies and the induction of the merit
system for personnel in the said local units of government ,
therefore-
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Two or more cities , or two or more
counties , or counties and cities together within the
Commonwealth of Kentucky , in order to promote
greater economy , may enter into an inter - city or
inter -county compact for the purpose of establish-
ing a single purchasing unit and the merit system .
Section 2. All of the expenses incurred due to
the operation of said inter - city or inter -county com-
pact shall be borne by the several cities or counties
who have entered into such a compact , and each
city or county shall bear its share of such expense
in proportion to its population in relationship to the
other cities or counties who have entered into said
compact . Such an inter -city or inter -county com-
pact unit shall make its own rules and regulations
subject to the limitations of the existing statutes
and further provisions of this Act .
Section 3. A. Each city or county shall desig-
nate some person who is a member of either the
Fiscal Court of the County, Council or Commis-
sion of the city to represent such city or county in
all of the business transactions by the inter -city or
inter -county commission according to the provisions
of this Act . Such a representative or commissioner
shall receive no compensation except in those cases
where traveling expense is involved .

The goal is to promote greater economy and efficiency in local government operations, particularly in light of mounting costs and the need for national defense efforts. Expenses for these compacts are to be shared proportionally based on population.

classification.title: AN ACT pertaining to Trial Commissioners to be appointed by county judges.

It authorizes county judges to appoint trial commissioners, who will hold office at the judge's pleasure and perform judicial duties assigned by the judge. The commissioner must have the qualifications of a county court judge and will receive a salary fixed by the fiscal court, not exceeding fifty dollars per annum, unless the county has a population of 200,000 or more, in which case the salary can be up to thirty-six hundred dollars per annum. The salary will be paid in the same manner as the county judge's salary.

CHAPTER 45

AN ACT relating to drainage districts .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
SECTION 1. If at any time any person or
persons owning lands within any drainage district
desires to withdraw his lands in whole or in part
from the drainage district , such person or persons
upon the approval of a majority of the qualified
members of the drainage board may petition the
County Court for a withdrawal of his or their lands
from the district . The judge shall docket the
petition upon the docket of his court which convenes
not less than fifteen days after the filing thereof
with him , and issue and cause to be served upon the
drainage board notice that, at the next term of his
court which convenes not less than ten days after
the service , he will consider the petition . Upon
proper showing that the lands should be withdrawn
from the drainage district , and if no valid reason is
shown against the withdrawal of the lands from
the drainage district , the judge shall enter an order
withdrawing the lands . After the order of with-
drawal is entered , the lands shall cease to be within
the drainage district , but an appeal may be taken
from the order of the circuit court , and from the
circuit court to the Court of Appeals . All cost in

AN ACT relating to commissioners of the Court of Appeals

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Section 962b - 1 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes , 1936 Edition is hereby amended and re-
enacted to read as follows :
The judges of the Court of Appeals , a majority of
them concurring , are hereby authorized to appoint
four commissioners , who shall hold office at the
pleasure of the court and who shall discharge such
duties as may be assigned to them by the court .
Every commissioner so appointed must have the
qualifications prescribed by law for a judge of the
Court of Appeals and as herein prescribed . Each
commissioner shall receive the same salary , to be
paid at the same time , and in the same manner , as a
judge of the Court of Appeals , and shall also be
allowed for clerical assistance the same amount
which is allowed by law to a judge of the Court of
Appeals . Each commissioner shall be provided
with an office in the Capitol at Frankfort , and the
same shall be furnished in a way suitable for his
duties by the Division of Purchases and Public
Properties , and the expenses therefor shall be paid
by a warrant to be drawn by the Department of
Finance upon the State Treasurer . When the busi-
ness of the court does not require their services the
court shall terminate the appointment of any or all
of the commissioners .

vided for , the judges of the Court of Appeals are
hereby authorized to , and may in their discretion ,

CHAPTER 56
(H. B. 128 )
AN ACT relating to the commission form of Government in
cities of the fifth and sixth class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Fifth and sixth class cities may re - organize
government under this law . That any city of the
fifth or sixth class , under the laws of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky may become organized and
governed under the provisions of this Act by pro-
ceeding as hereinafter provided . Organizing under
this act shall not change the corporate entity of any
such city , but the body politic and corporate shall
remain the same body that it now is .
§ 2. Existing laws and ordinances remain in
force where not in conflict herewith .-All laws
applicable to and governing cities of the fifth or
sixth class and not inconsistent with the provisions.
of this act shall continue to apply to and govern
each city that may organize under this act . And all
by-laws , ordinances and resolutions in force in any
such city and not inconsistent with the provisions of
this act , shall continue to be in force until altered or
repealed in manner provided for in this act .

CHAPTER 60
(H. B. 144 )
AN ACT to regulate the practice of dental hygiene , and vest-
ing the regulation thereof in the Kentucky State Board of
Dental Examiners .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Definitions.- ( a ) The term " dental
hygiene " as used in this act shall mean the treat-
ment of human teeth by removing therefrom cal-
careous deposits , and by removing accumulated ac
cretion from directly beneath the free margin of the
gums , and by polishing the exposed surface of the
teeth : Provided that nothing of this act shall be so
construed as to affect the practice of medicine or
dentistry nor to prevent students of a dental col-
lege or university from practicing dental hygiene
under supervision of their instructors .

360 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Public road districts may be estab-
lished in counties containing cities of the first class
for the purpose of providing the general public and
persons residing upon or owning property adjacent
to such roads with all -weather roads .
Section 2. A public road district may be estab-
lished in the following manner :
(a ) Any person or group of persons owning
property abutting upon any public road (which is
neither a County road nor a State road ) in counties
outside of and containing cities of the first class
may sponsor the creation of such a road district .
The sponsors shall first prepare or have prepared
for them a map of that section of such public road
which they desire to have improved . Such map shall
show the boundary lines of the road , the intersec-
tion with other roads and the terminal points on the
road desired to be improved, which shall always be
center lines of intersections with other roads , and
shall set forth on such map the names of the owners
of all property and the number of linear feet owned
by them abutting upon such road . The sponsors
of said road district shall also have estimated for
them by an engineer , who must be a private engineer
licensed by the Commonwealth of Kentucky , pur-
suant to Chapter 93 of the Acts of the General As-
sembly for the year 1928 , the approximate cost of
constructing the improvements desired , together
with a statement of the approximate cost which
shall be borne by each owner of property abutting
on the road, determined by the number of linear
feet of property owned by each abutting property
holder. Said sponsors of said road district shall
also prepare a petition and shall attach to said
petition the aforesaid maps and statements of cost .

§ 2. Intentional Injury To or Interference With
Property. Whoever intentionally destroys , im-
pairs , injures , interferes or tampers with real or
personal property with reasonable grounds to be-
lieve that such act will hinder , delay or interfere
with the prosecution by the United States or by any
of the States of defense or of war shall be punished
by imprisonment for not less than one ( 1 ) nor more
than ten ( 10 ) years , or by a fine of not more than
Ten Thousand ( $ 10,000 ) Dollars , or both .
If such act or acts be committed upon or about
real property or personal property in which are
housed , or about which there are reasonable grounds
to believe there are , human beings , whoever com-
mits such act or acts shall be punished by death in
the manner prescribed by the general law of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky .
§ 3. Intentionally Defective Workmanship .
Whoever intentionally makes or causes to be made
or omits to note on inspection any defect in any
article or thing with reasonable grounds to believe
that such article or thing is intended to be used in
connection with the prosecution by the United
States or by any of the States of defense or of war ,
or that such article or thing is one of a number of
similar articles or things , some of which are in-
tended so to be used , shall be punished by imprison-
ment for not less than one ( 1 ) nor more than ten
(10 ) years , or by a fine of not more than Ten Thou-
sand ( $ 10,000 ) Dollars , or both .

§ 12. Severability . If any provision of this Act
or the application thereof to any person or circum-
stances is held invalid , such invalidity shall not
affect other provisions or applications of the Act
which can be given effect without the invalid pro-
vision or application , and to this end the provisions
of this Act are declared to be severable .
$ 13. Short Title . This Act may be cited as the
Sabotage Prevention Act .
§ 14. Relation to Other Statutes . All acts and
parts of acts inconsistent with this Act are hereby
suspended in their application to any proceedings
under this Act. No word , phrase , sentence , para-
graph , or section of this Act shall suspend any law
now in existence in the Commonwealth of Kentucky
relating to the rights of Labor , nor shall any word ,
phrase , sentence , paragraph , or section of this Act
be construed as impairing , effecting , or abridging
the rights and grants of Labor . If conduct pro-
hibited by this Act is also made unlawful by an-
other or other laws , the offender may be convicted
for the violation of this Act or of such other law or
laws .
§ 15. Emergency Clause . Whereas , the Legisla-
ture is cognizant of the fact that the United States
is at war and a national emergency exists , making
necessary the enactment of law as set forth in this
Act,
Therefore , a state emergency is declared to exist ,
and this Act shall take effect immediately upon its
passage and approval by the Governor , and shall
continue in force until the President of the United
States declares the emergency at an end .
Approved 2nd March , 1942 .

AN ACT to provide for the conservation of wild animals , wildbirds , fish and amphibians .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Definitions . As used in this Act unless the
context requires otherwise :
(1 ) "Angling " means taking fish by hook and
line in hand , rod in hand , set- lines or trot lines , butdoes not include the use of grappling naked , snatchhooks , gang hooks , gigs or nets of any kind , or thetaking of fish where the bait or hook is not volun-tarily taken in the mouth by the fish .
(2 ) "Buy" includes offering to buy , acquiring
or possessing through purchase , barter , exchange
or trade .
(3) " Commission " means Game and Fish Ad-visory Commission .
(4 ) " Device " means any article , instrument orequipment of whatever nature or kind which may be
used to take wild animals , wild birds or fish .
(5) " Director " means Director of Game andFish Division .
(6) " Division " means Division of Game and
Fish in the Department of Conservation .
(7) " Minnows " means all fish under six inches
in length , except bass , rock bass , goggle -eye , trout ,
crappie, jack salmon , walleyed pike or striped bass ,
sand pike or sauger .
(8) " Navigable waters" means any waterswithin this state under lock and dam .
(9) " Nonresident " is a person who has not re-sided in this state for one year prior to his applica-tion for a license .

$ 40. Hunting deer , wild turkey or bear pro-
hibited . No person shall take , pursue , or molest
any elk , deer , wild turkey or bear .
§ 41. Traps ; type permitted ; permission of
landowner ; must be visited . ( 1 ) No person shall
set , use or maintain , for the capture of fur -bearing
animals , any trap , snare , or other device that does
not either take alive unhurt or kill at once . It shall
be lawful to use any leghold traps which take and
hold without pain or injury , any box or wire -cage
trap , the old - fashioned deadfall and the instant-
killing traps for very small animals . Instant -killing
traps shall be set within a burrow or hole , so as not
to injure domestic animals .
( 2 ) No person shall set traps on the land of an-
other person without written permission from the
owner of the land .
( 3 ) Each person who sets a trap must visit it at
least once in each twenty -four hours and release
any animal found therein .
§ 42. Permission to trap to be exhibited ; traps
to be tagged . No person shall set or attempt to set
any trap on the land of another without the written
permission of the owner in his possession and he
shall exhibit said written consent to anyone lawfully
demanding to examine it . Any trap set on the land
of another shall be marked with a metal tag , giving
the owner's name and place of residence .
43. Fur buyer's license ; issuance . ( 1 ) Be-
fore a person engages in the business of buying
furs and skins of fur -bearing animals , he shall se-
cure a license from the director .
( 2 ) No license shall be issued to a resident of a
state or county which does not grant equal priv-
ileges to citizens of this state .

$ 52. Restriction uponupon musseling operation .
A musseling license shall entitle the holder to op-
erate on any shellbed , but not more than four boats
may operate to the mile of shell bed .
$ 53. Report of musseling operations. On or
before December 31 of the year in which any license
is issued , the holder shall make a written report to
the Game and Fish Division , stating the total weight
of mussels taken under his license , the names and
location of the waters from which the mussels
were taken and the amount received for shells sold .
Upon the failure to make such report , the director
shall not issue another license until such report
is made .
$ 54. Division may regulate musseling . The
Game and Fish Division shall make such rules and
regulations governing the operation of boats in the
taking of mussels as it may deem best for the
proper enforcement of this chapter . To prevent
the depletion of the mussel beds and to insure
proper propagation of the mussels the Game and
Fish Division may close any beds to operators at any
time and for any period that it deems necessary .

activities who would not otherwise be able to secure such

investments and security for deposits ; and to declare an
emergency.
WHEREAS , it is hereby found and declared that the national-
defense program involves large increases in the military
forces and personnel in this Commonwealth , a great
increase in the number of workers in already established
manufacturing centers and the bringing of a large number
of workers and their families to new centers of defense
industries in the Commonwealth ; that there is an acute
shortage of safe and sanitary dwellings available to such
persons and their families which impedes the national-
defense program ; that it is imperative that action be taken
immediately to assure the availability of safe and sanitary
dwellings for such persons to enable the rapid expansion
of national - defense activities in this Commonwealth and to
avoid a large labor turnover in defense industries which
would seriously hamper their production ; that the pro-
visions hereinafter enacted are necessary to assure the
availability of safe and sanitary dwellings for persons

WHEREAS , it is hereby found and declared that the existence
and occupation of dwellings in cities of this Commonwealth
which are unfit for human habitation are inimical to the
welfare , and dangerous and injurious to the health , safety
and morals of the people of this Commonwealth , and that
a public necessity exists for the repair , closing or demolition
of such dwellings ; now , therefore :
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
$ 1. (1 ) Definitions and Construction . As
used in this Act , unless the context requires other .
wise :
(a ) " Dwelling " means any building , or struc-
ture , or part thereof , used and occupied for human
habitation or intended to be so used , and includes
any outhouses and appurtenances belonging thereto
or usually enjoyed therewith .
(b ) " Governing body " means the general coun-
cil , board, or commissioners , or other legislative
body , charged with governing a city .
(c ) " Public officer " means the officer or officers
who are authorized by ordinances adopted here-
under to exercise the powers prescribed by such
ordinances and by this Act .
(d ) " Public authority " means any Housing
Commission or any officer who is in charge of any
department or branch of the government of the city ,
county , or state relating to health , fire , building
regulations , or to other activities concerning dwell-
ings in the city .
(e ) " Owner " means the holder of the title in
fee simple and every mortgagee of record .
(f) " Parties in interest " means all individuals ,
associations and corporations who have interests .
of record in a dwelling and any who are in pos-
session thereof .

AN ACT providing for the disposition of property where

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the

SECTION 1. NO SUFFICIENT EVIDENCE
OF SURVIVORSHIP . Where the title to prop-
erty or the devolution thereof depends upon priority
of death and there is no sufficient evidence that the
persons have died otherwise than simultaneously ,

in this act .

classification.title: Uniform Simultaneous Death Act
classification.summary: This act establishes rules for distributing property when multiple beneficiaries die simultaneously, preventing disputes over survivorship. It outlines how property should be divided in cases of successive beneficiaries, joint tenants, tenants by the entirety, and insurance policies when the insured and beneficiary die at the same time. The act also specifies that it is not retroactive and does not apply if the decedent has made alternative provisions in their will, trusts, deeds, or insurance contracts.

CHAPTER 85 479
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
In counties having a population of 75,000 or
more , the salaries of the clerks of the respective
courts thereof, of sheriffs and of jailers and of their
deputies and all necessary office expenses , includ-
ing the equipping , furnishing , maintaining and op-
eration of said offices , shall be paid out of the state
treasury in amounts not to exceed 75 per cent of
the fees collected by said officers respectively , and
received into the treasury ; and 25 per cent of the
fees so collected by said officers respectively , and re-
ceived into the state treasury shall be paid in the
manner provided by law for the payment of other
claims against the state to the fiscal courts of said re-
spective counties . Said amount of 25 per cent of
said fees collected by said officers respectively dur-
ing each calendar year shall be paid to the fiscal
courts of the respective counties by April 1 of each
year succeeding the calendar year during which said.
fees were received by the Department of Finance .
This act shall become effective on July 1 , 1944 , and
shall apply only to fees properly received into the
custody of the said officers on and after that date .
Approved March 5 , 1942

484 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. The Governor shall , on or before March
1 every four years , beginning with 1942 , appoint a
Statute Revision Commission composed of four
members , to be selected from a list of eight persons ,
learned in the law , submitted by the Board of Com-
missioners of the Kentucky State Bar Association .
The members of the commission shall serve for
four years and until their successors are appointed
and have qualified . Vacancies in the commission
shall be filled by appointment by the Governor from
a list of two names for each vacancy submitted by
the Board of Commissioners of the Kentucky State
Bar Association . The members of the commission
shall receive no salaries , but shall receive their rea-
sonable and necessary expenses in connection with
the performance of their duties , to be paid out of
sums appropriated to the commission . The com-
mission shall constitute an agency independent of
the administrative departments provided for in
Chapter 12 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes .
§ 2. The Division of Purchases and Public Prop-
erties shall provide the commission with suitable
office space convenient to the State Law Library .
The State Librarian shall furnish the commission
with a complete set of the Kentucky Reports , begin-
ning with Volume 79 , and with advance sheets and
permanent volumes as they are published . The
State Librarian shall also furnish the commission
with a complete set of the printed Acts , beginning
with the Acts of 1891 , and with future volumes of
the Acts as published . The clerk of each house of
the General Assembly shall furnish to the commis-
sion a copy of each bill introduced in the house of
which he is clerk , as soon as the bill is introduced .
The contractor for state printing of the first class

2. Definitions , As used in this Act , unless the
content for such requires otherwise , the term ( 1 )
"Live Stock" shall mean sheep , cattle , hogs , horses ,
jacks , mules , poultry or any other animal or bird
commonly kept on the farm . ( 2 ) The term " Farmer '
shall mean any individual , firm , partnership , cor-
poration or farm management group which derives
a portion or all of its income from the production of
live stock domiciled on a farm within the Common-
wealth . ( 3 ) The term " Member " shall include
actual members of the association organized under
this Act. ( 4 ) The term " Association " means any
corporation organized under this Act . (5) The
term " Department " shall mean the Department of
Agriculture , Labor and Statistics .
Associations organized hereunder shall be termed
non -profit inasmuch as they are not organized to
make profit for themselves .
3. Who may organize . Any Twenty ( 20 ) or
more farmers whom are residents of one or not
more than five counties of this State , whether they
own the land they farm or not , may form a non-
profit Live Stock Protective Corporation without
capital stock under the provisions of this Act .
4. Purposes . An association may be organized
in any section of the State for the purpose of afford-
ing protection to the farmers in that locality in the
raising of live stock on their farms .

of the Commonwealth of Kentucky is hereby di-

CHAPTER 95
(H. B. 161 )
AN ACT adopting and putting into effect daylight saving time
throughout the Commonwealth of Kentucky by advancing
standard time one hour to run contemporaneously and for
a period of time provided by the Federal Daylight Saving
Act, and declaring an emergency .
WHEREAS , the National Government has adopted daylight
saving time in regard to interstate commerce and Federal
offices , agencies and works throughout the United States
and ,
WHEREAS , it is desirable to avoid confusion and conflict
that this Commonwealth and its subdivisions and agencies
in its various transactions be in accord with the Federal
Government in this matter .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the standard time as heretofore fixed and
existing in the United States pursuant to Act of
Congress and prior to the recent adoption of day .
light saving time during the period of the National
emergency be advanced by one hour , which time so
advanced shall apply to and govern all laws , regula-
tions and rules relating to the time or performance
of any Act by any officer or department of the Com-
monwealth , or of any county , city or subdivision or
agency thereof , or relating to the time that any
right shall accrue or determine , or within which any
act shall or shall not be performed by any person.

CHAPTER 96

AN ACT relating to probation and parole .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
1. There is hereby created in the Department of
Welfare a Division of Probation and Parole , headed
by a director who shall make rules and regulations
governing parolees and shall have the same powers

the functions vested in the Department of Welfare
relating to probation and parole .

classification.summary: This statute amends Section 979b-8 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, detailing the process by which a court can issue a warrant for the arrest of a defendant violating probation conditions. It also allows probation officers or other authorized officers to arrest a probationer without a warrant under specific circumstances, requiring a written statement from the probation officer as justification for detention until the probationer can be brought before the court. The court can revoke probation after a hearing or, in some cases, without a hearing if a certified copy of another court's order is filed.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the legal procedures for probation violations, including arrest and revocation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and procedures described are general to the criminal justice system and do not appear to be related to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses procedural aspects of probation, not racial segregation or discrimination. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to an act passed by the General Assembly in 1942 and discusses the enactment of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.

CHAPTER 97
(H. B. 192 )
AN ACT for the judicial notice of the laws of other jurisdic-
tions and for proof thereof and to make uniform the law
with reference thereto .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. JUDICIAL NOTICE . Every court
of this state shall take judicial notice of the com-
mon law and statutes of every state , territory and
other jurisdiction of the United States .
Section 2 . INFORMATION OF THE COURT .
The court may inform itself of such laws in such
manner as it may deem proper , and the court may
call upon counsel to aid it in obtaining such in-
formation .
Section 3. RULING REVIEWABLE . The de-
termination of such laws shall be made by the court
and not by the jury , and shall be reviewable .
Section 4 .4. EVIDENCE AS TO LAWS OF
OTHER JURISDICTIONS . Any party may also
present to the trial court any admissible evidence
of such laws , but , to enable a party to offer evidence
of the law in another jurisdiction or to ask that
judicial notice be taken thereof , reasonable notice
shall be given to the adverse parties either in the
pleadings or otherwise .

classification.title: AN ACT for the judicial notice of the laws of other jurisdictions and for proof thereof and to make uniform the law with reference thereto.

Parties can present evidence of foreign laws with reasonable notice to adverse parties.

WHEREAS a state of National emergency exists ,

CHAPTER 104
(H. B. 219 )
AN ACT authorizing clerks , sheriffs and jailers who are re-
quired to pay their fees into the State Treasury , to require
depositories to execute bonds or to pledge assets of such
banks as collateral security for deposits , providing the
terms of such bonds or pledges of collateral securities and
providing that such bonds or pledges shall not alter the
rights of the Commonwealth or county to proceed on the
officer's bond or against the banks individually .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Clerks , sheriffs and jailers who are
required under Section 106 of the Constitution to
pay the fees collected by them into the State Treas-
ury shall have the right , before naming any bank
as a depository or permitting such bank to receive
monies collected by them , to require the bank either
to execute a bond with good surety or pledge assets
to secure the deposits , as hereinafter set forth .
Section 2. If the bank elects to execute a bond ,
the bond shall be for a sum not less than the highest
anticipated amount which may be on deposit in the
bank at any time . The principal and sureties must
be worth jointly not less than double the face
amount of the bond . The bond shall be approved
by the officer requiring it to be executed and shall be
filed with and recorded by the county clerk .
Section 3. If the bank elects to secure the deposit
by the pledge of assets of the bank , the officer may
accept a pledge for that purpose of warrants of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky , bridge revenue bonds
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , United States
Government Bonds or such other bonds as are
guaranteed in full by the United States Govern
ment or any combination of such securities . The
pledge shall be in an amount at least ten percent in

classification.title: CHAPTER 104
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for banks acting as depositories for public funds, specifically for clerks of courts, sheriffs, and jailers. It details the collateral securities that must be pledged by the bank to secure these deposits, including market price adjustments and monthly revaluations. The statute also clarifies that the pledge of securities does not alter the existing liability of the officer or their sureties on their official bond, nor does it impair the Commonwealth or county's right to recover from the bank or its officers/stockholders in case of misapplication of funds or default. It also states that if an officer fails to require such a bond or pledge, they remain liable as an insurer of the funds.

AN ACT relating to attorneys for administrative depart-
ments , agencies , divisions and independent agencies of the

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky That :
Section 1. The term " department " as used in
this Act shall be construed to mean and to include
each and every administrative department , agency ,
division and independent agency , as said terms and
words are defined in the Governmental Re - Organ-
ization Act of 1936 .
Section 2. When any department deems it neces-
sary to employ an attorney or attorneys to render
any legal services for such department , the depart .
ment with the approval of the Governor shall have
the power and the authority to employ and fix the
terms of employment and the compensation to be
paid to the attorney or attorneys . The compensa-
tion and expenses of any attorney or attorneys em-
ployed under the provisions of this Act shall be
paid out of the appropriations made to such depart-
ment as other salaries , compensation and expenses
are paid . Before the employment of any attorney
under the provisions of this Act shall be made or
become effective , the Governor shall approve the
employment and the terms of employment by
Executive Order duly entered upon the Executive
Journal in the office of the Secretary of State .
An Attorney may be employed pursuant to the
provisions of this Act to render legal services for
one or more departments , and if employed by more
than one department he shall perform such services
as provided in the agreement entered into by the
departments and approved by the Governor . Ex-

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the

Section 1. The term " teacher " as used in this
Act shall not mean superintendent , principal , at-
tendance officer , supervisor or assistant superin-
tendent .
The term " year " as applied to terms of service
means actual service of not less than seven school
months within a school year , provided , however ,
that any board of education may grant a leave of
absence for professional advancement with full
credit for service .
The term " limited contract " shall mean a con-
tract for the employment of a teacher for a term of
four years or less .
The term " continuing contract " shall mean a
contract for the employment of a teacher which
shall remain in full force and effect until the
teacher resigns or retires or until it is terminated
or suspended as provided in this act , and shall be
granted only to a teacher holding a Standard or
College Certificate .
The term " continuing service status " means em-
ployment of a teacher under a continuing contract .
Section 2. Each board of education shall enter
into either limited or continuing contracts for the
employment of all teachers .
Section 3. Teachers eligible for continuing serv-
ice status in any school district shall be those
teachers qualified as to certification who have
taught for at least four years in the district , and
those teachers who , having attained continuing con-

Section 9. When by reason of decreased enroll-

CHAPTER 114 . 547
Whenever any warrant issued by the Department
of Finance is presented to the Treasurer for pay-
ment and the cash balance for such purpose does not
equal the amount on the face of the warrant , the
Treasurer shall make the warrant interest -bearing
as of the date it was presented for payment unless to
do so would exceed any statutory or constitutional
limitation as to the amount of interest-bearing state
warrants which may be issued or outstanding at any
one time . The Treasurer , with the written approval
of the Governor and the Commissioner of Finance ,
shall fix the interest at the lowest rate possible ,
taking into consideration the current money market ,
but the rate of interest shall not exceed five percent .
The Treasurer may contract with any purchaser or
holder of such warrant to accept interest at a lower
rate than that received by other holders . Interest-
bearing warrants shall draw interest from the date
they are stamped interest -bearing until they are
called for payment .
Sec . 2. Section 4688a -4 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes , 1936 edition , is amended to read as follows :
The State Treasurer , with the approval of the
Commissioner of Finance , shall have the right to
call interest -bearing warrants for payment at any
time he has sufficient funds on hand to pay said war-
rants . The call of the Treasurer shall be published
by him for ten consecutive days in a daily newspaper
published in this state , and no warrant so called in
shall bear interest after the call date specified in the
publication .
Sec . 3. The Commissioner of Finance shall pro-
vide the forms of all warrants issued under the pro-
visions of this Act .

enacted at the present session of the General Assem-
bly, sections one and two of this Act shall , upon the

Section 3780. COUNTY JUDGE TO APPOINT ;
JURISDICTION OF COUNTY POLICE .
The county courts of the respective counties shall
have and are hereby given the power, jurisdiction
and authority to establish , appoint and maintain a
county police force within their respective counties ,
all of the members and officers of which shall have
and are hereby given jurisdiction co -extensive with
the whole county for which they are appointed . Such
police force may consist of a chief and such member ,
rank and grade subordinate to the chief, and such
clerical and skilled employees as the county judge
shall deem proper . All of the members and employes
of the county police force shall be appointed by the
county judge and shall serve for a term of one year
and from the date of their respective appointments ,
unless sooner removed by the county judge for neg-
lect of duty or improper conduct . All members of
the force shall be citizens of the United States not
less than twenty -one years of age , and shall have
been bona fide residents of the county in which they
are appointed for not less than one year before their
appointment . None but discreet and sober persons
shall be appointed to any position on said county
police force . Each of the members of the said
county police force shall take an oath before the
judge of the county court of their county , to faith-
fully , impartially and diligently perform the duties
of their respective offices . Provided , however , that
the chief officer of the county police force of any
county may be designated , in the discretion of the
county court of said county , as captain or any other
appropriate title , and such county police force in any
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1942, p. 600

(H. B. 382 )
AN ACT to authorize individuals , partnerships , associations
or corporations organized , operating or doing business
under state laws to act as issuing agents for the sale and
issue of obligations of the United States upon designation
by the Secretary of the Treasury .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Notwithstanding the provisions of any
other law , all individuals , partnerships , associa-
tions or corporations organized , operating, or
doing business under the laws of this state are

emergency proclaimed by the President of the

AN ACT authorizing the Fiscal Court of any county to open,
establish or alter any public road or structure , providing
for the posting of certified copies of the Fiscal Court's order ,
and providing for appeals by persons aggrieved to the Cir-
cuit Court and to the Court of Appeals .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Whenever the Fiscal Court of any
county deems it to be in the best interest of such
county to open , establish or alter the location of
any public road, street , alley, ditch, culvert , bridge
or similar public way or structure in such county ,
said fiscal court shall adopt a resolution setting
forth the necessity for such public road or structure ,
and thereupon such public road or structure shall be
deemed opened , established or altered , as the case
may be , on behalf of the county . A certified copy of
said resolution or order shall be posted at the court-
house door of the county within five days after its
adoption and a certified copy of said resolution shall
be posted by the County Road Engineer of the county
along or at the proposed road or structure within
five days after its adoption.
Section 2. In all cases where such public roads
or structures have been established , any person or
persons aggrieved thereby may prosecute an appeal
from such order or resolution of the fiscal court by
filing a petition in equity in the circuit court of the

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. ( 1 ) Before any person's name shall
be placed upon the ballot at any primary or regu-
lar election as a candidate for the office of county
tax commissioner , he shall hold a certificate from the
Department of Revenue that he has been examined
by it and that he is qualified for the office . During
the month of January 1945 and every four years
thereafter , the Department of Revenue shall pre-
pare a list of questions to be used in the examina-
tion of applicants for a certificate . Such questions
shall be formulated so as to fairly test the ability
of the applicant to serve as county tax commis-
sioner . The Department of Revenue shall hold the
examination on the second Monday in March and ,
if there is only one person qualified to be a candi-
date in the county ; on the second Monday in June
beginning at 9 A. M. , at some convenient place
in at least one county seat town in each Congres-
sional District in the State . The Department of
Revenue shall advise the county Attorney of each
county of the time and place of each examination
and the county attorney shall post a notice thereof
in a conspicuous place in the courthouse three

pursuant to section 4154-1 of Baldwin's 1940 Sup-
plement , Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , such court
shall have authority to assess property which has
been omitted for any reason , whenever necessary
to establish the total personal liability of any de-
fendant in such action or to establish the total
amount of any lien or liens against the property .
Provided , however , that neither the twenty per cent
(20% ) penalty provided in section 4260-1 and sec-
tion 4263 of Baldwin's 1938 Supplement , Carroll's
Kentucky Statutes , nor the fifteen per cent ( 15 % )
administrative fee provided in section 4154-1 and
section 4154-2 of Baldwin's 1940 Supplement , Car-
roll's Kentucky Statutes , shall apply to the amount
of taxes , penalties and interest due for any assess-
ment made pursuant to this section for any omission
which was caused through no fault of the person
owning the property on the assessment date .

598 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That Section 3662a -3 of Kentucky
Statutes , Carroll's 1936 Edition , be amended to read
as follows :
Section 3662a -3. Defense may be made to the pe-
tition by any voter of the town , and if a defense is
made the court shall hear and determine the same
and may render judgment dissolving and annulling
the charter . The pleadings and practice , except as
herein provided , shall be the same as in equity
cases ; an appeal shall be from the judgment , pro-
vided the record is filed in the clerk's office of the
Court of Appeals twenty days prior to the second
term of the Court of Appeals after the rendition of
the judgment.
Whenever the charter of any town is dissolved in
the manner hereinabove provided , the judgment
shall direct the clerk of the circuit court wherein
such judgment is rendered , to certify a copy of said
judgment not later than ten days thereafter to the
Secretary of State whose duty it shall be to proper-
ly index and file the same as a permanent record in
his office .
Section 2. If the Kentucky Revised Statutes are
enacted at the present session of the General As-
sembly , section one of this Act shall , upon the taking
effect of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , be sub-
stituted in place of subsection ( 3 ) of KRS 88.020
and shall thereafter be treated as an amendment to
subsection (3 ) of KRS 88.020 rather than an
amendment to section 3662a -3 of Carroll's Statutes .
Approved 11th March , 1942 .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 3715 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes , 1936 edition , be amended to read as fol-
lows :
On the second day of the term the court shall , if
the proper notice has been given or publication
made , and no defense is interposed , enter a judg-
ment establishing a town in accordance with the
prayer of the petition , naming it and describing the
boundary thereof by metes and bounds , and ap-
pointing therefor not less than five trustees , a police
judge, marshal and assessor , who shall hold their
respective offices until the next election at which
officers for cities and towns may be elected . Defense
may be made to the petition by any inhabitant of the
proposed town , and if defense is made , the court
shall hear and determine the same , and render a
judgment establishing or refusing to establish a
town , as may seem proper . But if the court shall
be satisfied that the population within the pre-
scribed boundary is sufficient , and the proper notice
or publication has been made or given , it shall have
no discretion as to the establishment of the town .
The pleadings and practice , except as herein pro-
vided , shall be the same as in equity causes , but no
appeal shall lie from the judgment .
Whenever any town shall be established in the
manner above provided , the court shall in the judg-
ment direct the clerk of the court wherein such
judgment is entered to , not later than ten days
therafter , certify a copy thereof to the Secretary of

CHAPTER 134 .
(S. B. 147 )
AN ACT relating to county judges .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
$§ 1. Section 1072a of Carroll's Kentucky Stat-
utes , 1936 edition , is amended to read as follows :
The regular county judge in every county is en-
titled to a vacation of two weeks in each year and in
counties having a circuit court of continuous session
the county judge is entitled to a vacation of one
month in each year . During his vacation the com-
pensation of the pro tem judge shall not be deducted
from the salary of the regular judge . The salary of
the pro tem judge shall be paid by the county in the
same manner and at the same rate per diem as that
of the regular judge .
$§ 2. If the Kentucky Revised Statutes are en-
acted at the present session of the General Assem-
bly, section one of this Act shall , upon the taking
effect of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , be sub-
stituted in place of KRS 25.270 , and shall thereafter
be treated as an amendment to KRS 25.270 rather .
than an amendment to § 1072a of Carroll's Statutes .
Approved 11th March , 1942 .

CHAPTER 136 .
(S. B. 143 )
AN ACT relating to hours of labor on public work .
WHEREAS , our nation is now engaged in a conflict which
threatens its very existence ; a conflict which demands the
maximum of speed and efficiency in the construction and
maintenance of facilities of transportation ; and
WHEREAS , during this emergency, construction of these
defense facilities is greatly impeded by the requirement of
the eight hour day , and the forty hour week , and the pros-
ecution of our all - out war effort demands uninterrupted
construction of defense and other highways ; and
WHEREAS , the President of these United States has called
upon all industry to work twenty -four hours a day , seven
days a week , to produce the articles and facilities necessary
to defeat the enemies of Democracy ; and
WHEREAS , it is now unlawful to work more than eight hours
a day or forty hours a week on the construction of National
Defense Highways and Projects in Kentucky , now there-
fore , in order that we , in Kentucky , may do our full part in
this program ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :

CHAPTER 141 .
(S. B. 128 )
AN ACT amending and re - enacting Section 1649b - 3 of Car-
roll's Kentucky Statutes , Baldwin's Revision , relating to
the inadmissibility of confessions obtained by sweating .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 1649b - 3 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes , Baldwin's Revision of 1930 , be and the
same is amended and re -enacted , and as so amended
and re -enacted shall read as follows :
Section 1649b -3 . Not admissible . No confession
obtained by means of sweating , as defined herein ,
shall be permitted as evidence in any court in this
state , but same shall be deemed to have been ob-
tained by duress , if it be shown that such confession
was made after the arrest of the party charged
with crime and while he was in custody of the law.
The trial judge shall determine the competency and
admissibility of any alleged confession under the
provisions of this statute from evidence heard by
him , independent of and without the hearing of the
jury trying the case .

CHAPTER 142 617
1. An indictment , except in the cases mentioned
in Section 127 , much charge , but one offense , but
if it may have been committed in different modes
and by different means the indictment may allege
the modes , means or results in the disjunctive or
alternative .
2. No indictment for an offense created or de-
fined by statute shall be invalid or insufficient mere-
ly for the reason that it fails to negative any ex-
ception , excuse or proviso contained in the statute
creating or defining the offense .
3. An indictment need not allege that the of-
fense was committed or the act done " feloniously " ,
or " traitorously ", or " unlawfully " , or " with force
and arms " , or " with a strong hand " , nor need it
use any phrase of like kind otherwise to charac-
terize the offense ; nor need it allege that the of-
fense was committed or the act done " burglar-
iously " , " wilfully " , " knowingly " , " maliciously " ,
or "negligently " , nor need it otherwise character-
ize the manner of the commission of the offense ,
unless such characterization is necessary to charge
the offense under section 122 .
4. No indictment shall be held invalid because
the accusatory portion charges a common law of-
fense and the descriptive portion describes a stat-
utory offense , or the converse if the offenses are
of the same nature or are commonly known by sub-
stantially the same name .
5. The court may at any time cause the indict-
ment to be amended in respect of any defect , im-
perfection or omission in the matter of form only.
Approved 11th March , 1942 .
