Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
1. The observance of all holidays other than In-
dependence Day , Labor Day , and Christmas Day
shall be suspended during the continuation of hostil-
ities in the present war with Japan , Germany , Italy ,
and any other enemies of the United States who
may become involved therein .
2. The observance of holidays now provided for
by law shall be renewed after the cessation of such
hostilities ; and upon the apparent permanency of
such cessation of hostilities , the Governor of the
Commonwealth shall issue a proclamation so de-
claring ; and thereupon the holidays now provided
by law shall again be observed .
3. Because of the urgent necessity for the con-
tinuous application of everyone to the existing war
effort of the United States , an emergency is de-
clared to exist and this act shall take effect on and
after its passage and approval by the Governor .

(d) "Operator " means an individual , firm or

§ 2. The ventilation of all clay mines at which
as many as five men are employed shall be pro-
duced by means of fans mechanically operated ,
located inside or outside the mine . Mines at which
less than five men are employed shall be ventilated
by the same means whose specifications have been
approved by the Department of Mines and Miner-
als for coal mines .
§ 3. In case of accident to the ventilating fan ,
or its machinery whereby the ventilation of the mine
would be seriously interrupted , the mine foreman
shall order the men to withdraw immediately from
the mine and he shall not allow them to return to
their work until the ventilation has been restored
and the mine has been thoroughly examined by him ,
or by an assistant mine foreman or fire boss , and
the mine is reported safe .

man of every clay mine , whether worked by shaft ,

ute for each and every person employed in the por-

quirement ; provided , further , that while the repair

sons shall be permitted to enter that part of the
mine affected except those actually employed in
doing the necessary repair work , that repair work
to be done under the constant supervision of an

CHAPTER 165
(H. B. 148 )
AN ACT relating to credit for imprisonment during inability
to secure bail .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Section 1142a of Carroll's Kentucky Stat-
utes , 1936 edition , is amended to read as follows :
If in any trial in a circuit , county , quarterly , jus-
tice's or police court of this state , the defendant has
been compelled to remain in jail , by reason of his
inability to execute bond , to answer the charge in
the court in which he is held, and , on trial had in
such court, he has been found guilty and his punish-
ment fixed at a fine or a term in the county jail , he
shall, in the case of a fine , be given credit at the rate
of two dollars per day for each day he was so com-
pelled to remain in jail , and in the case of a jail sen-
tence , shall be given credit on such sentence for the
time he was so compelled to remain in jail . The
court shall endorse on the judgment a credit for the
number of days the defendant was so compelled to
remain in jail , and , in the case of a jail sentence ,
shall make an entry on the commitment , and the de-
fendant shall be discharged from the county jail
when he has served the full term for which he was
convicted less such credit of time as he is to receive
as herein provided .
2. If the Kentucky Revised Statutes are
enacted at the present session of the General As-
sembly , section one of this Act shall , upon the tak-
ing effect of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , be sub-
stituted in place of KRS 431.150 and shall there
after be treated as an amendment to KRS 431.150
rather than an amendment to § 1142a of Carroll's
Statutes .
Approved March 11 , 1942 .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1942, p. 708

classification.title: AN ACT relating to credit for imprisonment during inability to secure bail.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 1142a of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edition, to provide credit for time spent in jail due to inability to post bond. Upon the application of any carrier by railroad ,

ice by railroad has been and is being carried on at a

or whenever any passenger service by railroad has
for any reason become unnecessary in the public
interest ; and no carrier by railroad shall discontinue
any passenger train or substitute a mixed train for
a passenger train on any line of railroad or branch
thereof until said Railroad Commission shall have
authorized the discontinuance or substitution in
accordance with the provisions of this section , pro-
vided , however , this clause shall not apply to sea-
sonal passenger trains .
§ 2. If the Kentucky Revised Statutes are

sembly , section one of this Act shall, upon the taking

classification.summary: This act amends a statute concerning railroad passenger service. It grants the Railroad Commission of Kentucky the power to authorize the discontinuation of passenger service on any railroad line or branch if it is operating at a loss with no reasonable prospect of improvement, or if the service has become unnecessary in the public interest. No carrier can discontinue or substitute a mixed train for a passenger train without the Commission's authorization, except for seasonal passenger trains.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the

1. Definition . The term " fiduciary " as used in
this act means any person , association , or corpora-
tion ( other than assignee or trustee for an insolvent
debtor , or a guardian under the uniform veterans '
guardianship act ) appointed by , or under the con-
trol of , or accountable to the county court , includ-
ing executors , administrators , administrators with
the will annexed , testamentary trustees , curators ,
guardians and committees .
2. Who May Be Appointed . Any resident of
the State of Kentucky , including married women ,
over 21 years of age and any National Bank located
in Kentucky having fiduciary powers and any State
Bank or Trust Company incorporated under the
Laws of the State of Kentucky and authorized by
law to act as such therein may be appointed as
fiduciary .
3. Appointment and Duties . Every fiduciary ,
before entering upon the execution of the trust ,
shall receive letters of appointment from the county
court having jurisdiction as now fixed by law . The
duties of a fiduciary shall be such as are required
by law , and such additional duties not inconsistent
therewith as the court may order
4. Representation by Attorney . At the time of
appointment of a fiduciary , he may designate an
attorney who will represent him in matters relating
to the trust and when so designated , notices to such
fiduciary shall also be sent by the court to such
attorney , but no fiduciary shall be required to be
represented by attorney .

a suit to settle the estate such neglect or refusal

justified .
Duties of Clerk and Judge . It shall be the duty
of the clerk to report in writing to the judge once
each month the names and addresses of all fidu-
ciaries who have during the previous calendar
month become delinquent in filing an inventory or
settlement . The judge shall notify by mail such
delinquent fiduciaries and shall warn them of the
penalties provided by law . If the clerk shall fail
to report such delinquents it shall be the duty of the
judge to secure a list of same from the court records .
There shall be taxed as costs in each delinquent case
a fee of Twenty -Five ( $ .25 ) Cents for the prepara-
tion of said list and the mailing of said notice , to be
divided between the clerk and the judge . If the

classification.summary: This act amends existing legislation regarding the hours of operation for establishments licensed to sell distilled spirits and wine at retail. It prohibits sales between midnight and 8 a.m., and at any time on Sundays or election days. Furthermore, it grants city councils in first, second, and third-class cities the exclusive right to establish specific sales hours within their jurisdictions.

after given, and the devices named and lists of the

It also outlines how to handle ballots where a voter marks candidates from different parties.

cuit judge of the county , sell the material for the

county treasury , after taking proper precautions to
see that no information as to how any voter has

terial . If no sale can be made the material shall be

which a person in military service is absent from
duty on account of sickness , wounds , leave or other
lawful causes .
(3 ) "Period of military service " includes the
time between the following dates : For persons in
active service at the date of the approval of this
act it shall begin with the date of approval of this
act ; for persons entering active service after the
approval of this act with the date of their entering
active service . It shall terminate with the date of
discharge from active service or death while in
active service but in no case later than the date when
this act ceases to be in force .
(4) "Termination of the War " means termina-
tion of the present War by treaty of peace as pro-
claimed by the President .

CHAPTER 171
(S. B. 197 )
AN ACT to amend Section 246 of the Constitution of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky relating to compensation for
official services .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That Section 246 of the Constitution
of Kentucky be and the same hereby is amended by
repealing said section in its entirety and enacting
in lieu thereof the following .
Section 2. The General Assembly, as soon as
practicable after the enactment of this amendment ,
shall regulate compensation of public officials and
employees , including those in office and those elected
to office at the time of adoption of this amendment ;
and provide for penalties and what deductions shall
be made for neglect of official duties . Thereafter ,
the compensation of public officials shall not be
increased or decreased during the terms for which
they are elected or appointed .
Section 3. This amendment shall be submitted to
the voters of the State for their ratification or re-
jection at the time and in the manner provided for
under Sections 256 and 257 of the Constitution of
Kentucky , and under the provisions of Section 1459
Kentucky Statutes , Baldwin's 1936 Revision and
K. S. R. Section

CHAPTER 172
( S. B. 184 )
AN ACT relating to building and loan associations .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. ( 1 ) Whenever it appears that the
capital stock of any building and loan assocation
has been impaired , the Director of Banking shall
notify such building and loan association and each
director thereof to make such impairment good
within thirty days and to restrict withdrawals and
not to accept unpledged shares . The officers and
directors shall immediately call a special meeting
of the shareholders . The association may , with the
consent of the Director of Banking , reduce its capi-
tal to the extent of the impairment , if such reduc-
tion will not place its capital below the amount
required by law.

CHAPTER 173
(H. B. 277)
AN ACT relating to fiscal courts and amending and con-
solidating §§ 1833 and 1836 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes ,
1936 edition .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Sections 1833 and 1836 of Carroll's Ken-
tucky Statutes , 1936 edition , are consolidated and
amended to read as follows :
(1 ) The fiscal court of each county shall consist
of the county judge , or in his absence the county
judge pro tem , and the justices of the peace of the
county ; or of the county judge , or in his absence
the county judge pro tem , and three county com-
missioners elected under §§ 1847 to 1849 or
§ 1851b - 1 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes .
( 2 ) The county judge, or in his absence the
county judge pro tem , shall preside , if present . If
neither the county judge nor the county judge pro
tem is present or cannot preside , a majority of the
justices of the peace or commissioners shall elect
one of their number to preside during the absence
or inability of the county judge or county judge pro
tem to preside . The county judge , or the county
judge pro tem or justice presiding in his place , shall
preserve order , and may fine and imprison for con-
tempt of court in the same manner as the presiding
judge of the county court.
§ 2. If the Kentucky Revised Statutes are en-
acted at the present session of the General Assem-
bly , section one of this Act shall, upon the taking
effect of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , be sub-
stituted in place of subsections ( 1 ) and ( 2 ) of KRS
67.040 , and shall thereafter be treated as an amend-
ment to subsections ( 1 ) and ( 2 ) of KRS 67.040
rather than an amendment to §§ 1833 and 1836 of

classification.title: AN ACT relating to fiscal courts and amending and consolidating §§ 1833 and 1836 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edition.
classification.summary: This act consolidates and amends sections 1833 and 1836 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edition, concerning the composition and presiding officers of county fiscal courts. It specifies that the fiscal court shall consist of the county judge (or judge pro tem) and either justices of the peace or three elected county commissioners. The county judge or their substitute presides, with provisions for electing a presiding officer from among the justices or commissioners if the judge is absent or unable to preside. The presiding officer has the power to preserve order and punish contempt.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
" Subsection ( e ) of Section 2 of Chapter 13 of the
1940 Acts of the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky be amended so that when

calculated on the actual price inclusive of bottling

taxes levied by the Federal Government subsequent
to the first day of March , 1942. "

classification.title: An Act relating to city and county planning and zoning commissions and boards of adjustment and appeals.
classification.summary: This act authorizes cities of the first class and their respective counties to enter into agreements to create joint planning and zoning commissions and boards of adjustment and appeals. The purpose is to regulate the physical development of incorporated and unincorporated areas for the promotion of public health, safety, morals, and general welfare. It outlines the creation, organization, powers, duties, financing, rules, regulations, and procedures for these joint bodies, as well as methods for adopting, recording, and enforcing plans. It also provides for the abolishment of existing commissions and boards, transfer of records, subdivision control, and penalties for violations. The act repeals several sections of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, Annotated (Baldwin's 1936 Revision) related to planning and zoning.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with urban and regional planning and zoning. It aims to regulate physical development for public health, safety, and welfare. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit mechanisms that would suggest racial discrimination. The language is focused on land use and development. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act enacted by the General Assembly' in 1942.

classification.title: Zoning Regulations for Counties and Cities
classification.summary: This statute grants counties and cities the power to enact zoning regulations concerning land use, building erection, and the future location and width of streets and highways. It specifies that these regulations generally do not apply to land used solely for agricultural purposes, unless a portion is used for commercial or business purposes. The statute also allows for the division of areas into districts with uniform regulations within each class but allows for differences between districts. It permits the continuation of lawful non-conforming uses of land existing at the time of zoning adoption, provided the use is not discontinued for a year without efforts to resume it or lease it for a similar or more restricted use.

It is unlawful to erect, construct, reconstruct, alter, or change the use of any building or land without obtaining a permit or certificate, which will only be issued if the plans conform to existing zoning regulations. Permits for building on private property require access to a public street, highway, or road.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and enforcement mechanisms for zoning regulations. It focuses on the process of obtaining permits for construction and land use changes, ensuring compliance with established zoning rules. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly create racial distinctions or segregation. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

sembly , 1938 , First Extraordinary Session , is here-
by repealed , amended and re -enacted so that when

Section 6. Sale of County Bonds -The order
of the fiscal court of any county providing for the
issue or reissue and sale of any bonds shall specify :
(a) the maximum amount to be issued , ( b ) the de-
nominations in which the bonds shall be issued , ( c )
the maximum interest rate to be paid on the bonds ,
(d ) the term of the bonds ( if serial , a fully de-
tailed principal maturity schedule shall be shown ) ,
and such other information as the Commission may
by regulation require . A certified copy of such
order shall be submitted to the State Local Finance
Officer , who shall expedite approval or disapproval
if requested or if required by this Act .
All county bonds offered for sale shall be adver-
tised for sealed competitive bids at least once dur-
ing each of two consecutive weeks in a newspaper
of general bona fide circulation in Kentucky and in
the county offering the bonds for sale , and at least
once in a periodical having general circulation
among bond buyers , and shall be sold by the fiscal
court to the bidder at such sale offering the price
and interest rate most advantageous to the county .

classification.summary: This act amends Section 9 of Chapter 31 of the Acts of the General Assembly, 1938, First Extraordinary Session. It grants the State Local Finance Officer, with Commission approval, and the fiscal court authority to invest county sinking funds not immediately needed for debt obligations. Investments can be made in obligations secured by the U.S. Government, Commonwealth of Kentucky warrants, or county road and bridge bonds approved by the Commission, provided securities mature before funds are needed. It also allows counties or the State Local Finance Officer to purchase their own bonds at a discount prior to maturity using sinking fund monies for savings.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the management and investment of county sinking funds. It outlines the authority of the State Local Finance Officer and fiscal courts to invest these funds in government-backed securities or local bonds. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1942. The confidence is high because the content is clearly financial and administrative, with no apparent racial implications.

Sec . 1083a -4-Recorders and Deputy Recorders ;
employment of ; term ; compensation ; bonds- In
each justice's district of a county having a popula-
tion of over 250,000 the justice of the peace shall
employ one clerical assistant to be known as the
recorder , whose employment shall continue for the
period during which the justice of the peace making
the appointment holds office , but not beyond the
current term of office of said justice . Each justice
of the peace may employ not exceeding three clerical
assistants known as deputy recorders . Each re-
corder and each deputy recorder shall continue to
be compensated for his services by salaries now
fixed by law and paid out of the County levy ; pro-
vided, however , that such salaries may be increased
or decreased to the amount fixed by the justice of
the peace by written recommendation to the Fiscal
Court of the County , which recommendation shall
become effective upon its approval by resolution
of the Fiscal Court . The said salaries shall be in
lieu of all other compensation . The Fiscal Court
of the county shall require bonds , with good and
sufficient sureties , of recorders and deputy record-
ers , for the faithful performance of their duties .
The employment of each recorder and each deputy
recorder shall be noted of record by the justice of
the peace by the entry of a suitable order upon the
records of the court, and the employment of any
recorder or deputy recorder may be terminated at
any time , for cause , by order of record by the justice
of the peace holding office within the district . Any
of said employees shall be eligible to serve in any
justice's district in the county .

The Official Reporters of the Chancery Branch of
Circuit Courts in counties having a population of

the Fiscal Court , either a fixed annual salary , to be
paid in monthly installments out of the county levy ,
or shall be paid a guaranteed annual amount , pay-
able in equal monthly installments out of the county
levy . In the event that the Fiscal Court elects to
pay a fixed annual salary to said reporters , said
reporters shall not be required to repay to the
county said salary or any part of the fees collected
by them . In the event that the Fiscal Court elects
to pay such reporters a guaranteed annual amount
in lieu of a fixed salary, said reporters shall settle
monthly with the Fiscal Court of the County for
fees collected by such reporters and shall pay the
amount found to be due on said settlement im-
mediately into the County Treasury until they have
paid into the County Treasury during any calendar
year an amount equal to the guaranteed annual
amount paid to them by the Fiscal Court ; provided ,
however , that said reporters shall in no event be
required to reimburse the County for sums received
by them beyond the amount which said reporters
shall have collected from the County in their
respective offices during said calendar year , and
they may retain any said excess without accounting
to the County therefor . If any of said reporters of
the Chancery Branch are employed for less than one
year , they shall be required to account to the
County only for so much of the fees collected by
them during that time as shall equal the sums paid

reporters collect less than the amounts they are

The amendments primarily concern the salaries of stenographers and the appointment of auditors and assistant auditors in counties, particularly those containing a city of the first class. The salary of a stenographer can be adjusted by the County Judge with Fiscal Court approval, with a cap of $1,500 per annum in counties not containing a first-class city. § 4. No make of voting machine shall be ap-
proved for use unless it is so constructed that :
(1 ) It will insure secrecy to the voter in the act

cial or primary election , and for or against any pub-

for one or more candidates of every party having

vote for , and no more .

§ 13. If , during the conduct of an election , a ma-

not be operated in a manner that will comply with
the provisions of this Act , the election officers shall
lock or seal the machine in such a manner as to pre-
vent further voting thereon and record the numbers
shown by the public counter . Then the election
officers shall secure from the county clerk a reserve
voting machine , which shall be repaired and made
ready for use as provided in section 12 , and there-
upon proceed to conduct the election . When the polls
are closed both the original and reserve voting ma-
chines shall be examined and the votes thereon reg-
istered shall be counted as provided in section 15
and the aggregate number of votes cast on both ma-
chines for each candidate and on each question shall
be certified as the result of the election in that pre-
cinct .

Any party aggrieved by the action of the Commis-

Circuit Court , with good and solvent surety . The
Commission shall be a necessary party to all such
appeals . The Circuit Court Clerk shall thereupon
docket the case as though it were a petition in equity
and shall immediately issue a summons for such
Commission , and said summons shall be returned in
the same manner as are summonses in equity cases .
No formal pleadings shall be required in such ap-
peals but the case shall be set down by the Court for
as early a date as possible for a hearing, and after
such hearing the Court shall enter a judgment
sustaining or setting aside the order of the Commis-
sion . Either party may appeal to the Court of
Appeals from the judgment of the Circuit Court in
the same manner as an appeal can be taken from a
judgment in equity cases .

828 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
DEFINITIONS AND APPLICATION
281.010 ( 2739j - 1 ; 2739j -42 ; 2739j -94 ; 2739L -9 ;
27390-1 ) Definitions . As used in this chapter , unless
the context requires otherwise :
( 1 ) " Certificate " means a certificate of public
convenience and necessity authorized to be issued
under KRS 281.090 .
(2 ) "City bus " means any motor vehicle used
for the transportation of persons for hire exclus-
ively within the limits of a city or within ten miles
of its limits , over a regular route .
( 3) " Common carrier " means any operator of
a motor vehicle for hire in common carriage other
than the operator of a taxicab or city bus , except
that the operator of a city bus who obtains a certifi-
cate under subsection ( 2 ) of KRS 281.040 shall
thereupon become a common carrier as to that por-
tion of its operation covered by such certificate .
(4) "Contract carrier " means any person who ,
under individual contracts or agreements , engages
in the transportation ( other than as a common car-
rier ) by motor vehicle of passengers or property
for hire .
(5 ) " Director " means the Director of the Di-
vision of Motor Transportation .
(6 ) " Division " means the Division of Motor
Transportation in the Department of Business Reg-
ulation .
(7) " Motor Vehicle " means any motor -propelled
vehicle used for the transportation of persons or
property on the public highways , including any
such vehicle operated as a unit in combination with
other vehicles , but not including vehicles propelled
by electric power obtained from overhead wires ,
and not including vehicles usually operated over
or on rails .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1942, p. 879

CHAPTER 185 829
( 8 ) " Operator of a motor vehicle for hire ”
means any person who owns , controls , operates or
manages any motor vehicle for hire for the trans-
portation of persons or property on any public
highway , or any person who engages in the business
of leasing motor vehicles for a compensation for
the transportation of persons or property upon the
public highways, but does not include any person
who transports only his own property .
(9) "Permit" means a permit to operate a
motor vehicle as a contract carrier authorized to be
issued under KRS 281.220 .
(10 ) " Public highway " means any public street ,
alley , road or highway in this State , whether within
or without the corporate limits of any city .
( 11 ) "Taxicab " means any motor vehicle de-
signed or constructed to accommodate or transport
passengers , not more than six in number exclusive
of the driver , the principal operation of which is
confined to the corporate limits and suburban area
of cities of this State , and not operating over any
definite route , and the destination of which is des--
ignated by the passenger or passengers at the time
of such transportation .
( 12 ) " Suburban area" means territory wholly
within the State of Kentucky which extends not
more than one mile beyond the corporate limits of
any municipality in this State and one mile addi-
tional for each fifty thousand population or major
fraction thereof .
( 13 ) "Occasional or casual trips " means the
occasional transportation of passengers or the prop-
erty of others for hire by one who does not regu-
larly engage in the transportation business in this
State nor operate under a certificate between fixed
termini or over a regular route .

classification.title: Exemptions to Motor Vehicle Regulations
classification.summary: This statute outlines specific exemptions from certain motor vehicle regulations. It exempts vehicles used for transportation of property for hire within city limits or suburban areas, school buses transporting students under school authority, vehicles used by non-profit cooperatives for property transportation, vehicles transporting agricultural and dairy products from the farm to market, church buses for religious work, and trucks hauling highway construction materials. The definition of 'producer' and 'agricultural products' is also clarified.
classification.reasoning: The statute details exemptions for various types of motor vehicle usage, such as school buses, agricultural transport, and church buses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is purely on the type of service or goods being transported and the entities involved. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

281.030 ( 2739j - 95 ; 2739k - 1 ; 2739k -2 ) Regula-
tion of interstate commerce . ( 1 ) The provisions
of this chapter shall apply to interstate commerce ,
except in so far as such application is in conflict
with any provision of the constitution of the United
States or the Acts of Congress .
( 2 ) The director of the Division of Motor Trans-
portation may grant a certificate of convenience and
necessity for the transportation of persons or prop-
erty for compensation in interstate commerce , and
may regulate such interstate commerce under the
authority of, and in accordance with , the pro-
visions of any Act of Congress vesting in or dele-
gating to the Director such authority , as an agency
of the United States Government . The Director
may notify the proper department of the Federal
Government of his assent to the requirements and
conditions of such Act of Congress in regard to
interstate commerce by motor vehicle .

281.110 ( 2739j -71 ) Amendment of certificate
transfer . (1 ) Applications for amendments to cer-
tificates may be made in the same manner as an ap-
plication for an original certificate , and may be
granted in the same manner as provided for an
original certificate .
(2 ) Any certificate owned , held or obtained by
any carrier may be sold , assigned , leased , be-
queathed or transferred as other personal property
upon approval by the Division of Motor Transporta-
tion , which may inquire into the responsibility of the
person obtaining or seeking to obtain ownership or
control of any certificate , and if it finds such person
to be irresponsible or unable to render satisfactory
and adequate service under said certificate it may
enter an order denying the transfer ; provided , how-
ever , that no certificate may be sold , assigned , or
leased , bequeathed or transferred except after a
public hearing and after notice as required for other
hearings .
281.120 ( 2739j - 72 ; 2739j -82 ) Duration of certifi-
cate ; renewal ; cancellation . No operating rights
under a certificate shall be effective after January 1
of any year unless extended for the subsequent year
by the payment to the division of the fees prescribed
for original applications . Evidence of the extension
of operating rights and a receipt for the fees thereof
shall be issued in a form prescribed by the division .
It may refuse to accept extension fees for any cer-
tificate only for just cause and may cancel any cer-
tificate for any just cause other than those named in
KRS 281.130 but only after a hearing , thirty days '
notice of which shall be given to the holder of the
certificate .

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
classification.summary: This statute, specifically sections 281.180 and 281.190, outlines the powers of the Division of Motor Transportation in Kentucky. It grants the division authority to fix or approve rates, fares, charges, classifications, rules, and regulations for common carriers. It also allows the division to regulate operating schedules to ensure adequate and convenient transportation service. Furthermore, it details the process for the division to determine just and reasonable rates if existing ones are found to be excessive, unreasonable, or discriminatory, or if services are unsafe, inadequate, or in violation of law. The statute also addresses the abandonment or change of routes or services by common carriers, requiring an order from the division based on public convenience and necessity.

classification.title: Working hours of drivers and chauffeurs.
classification.summary: This statute, enacted in 1942, regulates the working hours of drivers and chauffeurs operating motor vehicles for hire. It prohibits common carriers or contract carriers from requiring or permitting drivers to be continuously on duty for more than twelve hours, mandating at least eight consecutive hours off duty. It also limits total duty to sixteen hours in any twenty-four hour period, requiring at least ten consecutive hours off duty after that. The periods off duty must be sufficient for rest and relaxation, and a period off duty is only considered to break continuity of service if it is at least three consecutive hours and taken in a suitable location.

classification.title: An Act relating to the transfer and management of special funds in counties and cities.
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for handling surplus funds from special levies in Kentucky counties and cities. If a special purpose for which a tax was levied is accomplished, any remaining funds are to be transferred to the general revenue fund. However, if these funds can be identified and have not been commingled, the county or city may return them to a special reserve fund for a similar purpose and invest them until needed. The act also includes provisions for the repeal of Section 1 if the Kentucky Revised Statutes are enacted during the same session, with corresponding amendments to KRS 68.120 and KRS 92.360.

CHAPTER 198.
(S. B. 170 )
AN ACT relating to distilled spirits .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That any person or persons owning any distilled
spirits which were manufactured prior to National
Prohibition and upon which all Federal taxes have
been paid shall have the right to withdraw same for
his own personal use without the payment of the
Kentucky Consumer's Tax or any other excise
taxes , and the proprietor of any free warehouse or
of any distillery shall deliver such distilled spirits
to the owner thereof.
Any person so owning such distilled spirits shall
before the removal of same from any distillery or
free warehouse execute an affidavit and file it with
the Administrator of the Distilled Spirits Unit in
the Department of Revenue to the effect that such
distilled spirits will be used only for personal con-
sumption and are not to be withdrawn for purposes
of re - sale and will not be placed in the ordinary
channels of trade.

3. Against defendant constructively summoned .
Allegations against a defendant constructively sum-
moned , who has not appeared in the action .
4. Concerning value or amount of damages .
Allegations concerning value or amount of damage ,
not accompanied by an allegation of an express
promise , or by a statement of facts showing an im-
plied promise , to pay such value or damage ; such
allegations , so accompanied , need not be proved
unless traversed .
§ 6. Sections 99 , 100 and 101 of the Civil Code
of Practice are repealed .
Approved by Governor , March 13 , 1942 .

CHAPTER 201 889
§ 1. Section 4377-8 of Carroll's Kentucky Stat-
utes , 1936 edition , is amended to read as follows :
A majority of the board shall constitute a quorum
for the transaction of business . The board shall
meet every three months and at such other times as
it may be called as provided in this section . Special
meetings of the board may be called by the chair-
man , and upon written request of three members
the chairman shall call a special meeting of the
board . The chairman shall give notice through the
secretary by registered mail to each member of the
board at least ten days prior to the time of any
meeting , unless all members of the board waive
notice in writing . The offices of the board shall be
at the seat of government in the Department of
Education and shall be provided by the state . Mem-
bers of the board may receive a per diem of ten
dollars for each regular or special meeting attended
and actual expenses for attending meetings , and
may be reimbursed for other actual and necessary
expenditures incurred in the state in the per-
formance of their duties authorized by the board.
The per diem and expenses shall be paid out of the
appropriation for the State Board of Education .
However no member of said Board shall receive
more than $ 100.00 per annum for per diem under
the provisions of this act .
§ 2. If the Kentucky Revised Statutes are en-
acted at the present session of the General Assem-
bly , section one of this Act shall , upon the taking
effect of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , be sub-
stituted in place of KRS 156.060 , and shall there-
after be treated as an amendment to KRS 156.060
rather than an amendment to § 4377-8 of Carroll's
Statutes .
Not approved .

904 . ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That Section seventeen hundred sixty-
three of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , Baldwin's
1936 Revision , be repealed and that the following
new matter be enacted in lieu thereof .
( 1 ) In counties having a population of two hun-
dred thousand ( 200,000 ) or over the salary of the
chief deputy sheriff shall be fixed at a reasonable
amount not exceeding three thousand dollars
($3,000.00 ) a year . The salary of one deputy sheriff
who shall act as process clerk shall be fixed at a
reasonable amount not to exceed twenty-five hun-
dred dollars ( $2,500.00 ) a year and the salary of
each of the other deputy sheriffs shall be fixed at
a reasonable amount not exceeding two thousand
dollars ( $2,000.00 ) a year .
( 2 ) In counties having a population of two hun-
dred thousand ( 200,000 ) or over the salary of the
chief deputy circuit court clerk shall be fixed at a
reasonable amount not exceeding three thousand
dollars ($3,000.00 ) a year , the salary of one deputy
circuit court clerk who shall act as cashier shall be
fixed at a reasonable amount not exceeding twenty-
five hundred dollars ( $2,500.00 ) a year , and the sal-
ary of each of the other deputies in the office of the
circuit court clerk shall be fixed at a reasonable
amount not exceeding two thousand dollars
($2,000.00 ) a year .
( 3 ) In counties having a population of two hun-
dred thousand ( 200,000 ) or over the salaries of the
first four deputy county court clerks shall be fixed
at a reasonable amount not exceeding three thou-
sand dollars ( $3,000.00 ) a year and the salary of
each of the other deputies in the office of the county
court clerk shall be fixed at a reasonable amount not
exceeding two thousand dollars ( $2,000.00 ) a year .

When a cause of action has arisen in another

country where the cause of action accrued the time
for the commencement of an action thereon is lim-
ited to a shorter period of time than the period of

in this state at the expiration of said shorter period .

sembly , section one of this Act shall , upon the tak-
ing effect of the Kentucky Statutes , be substituted
in place of KRS 413.320 , and thereafter be treated
as an amendment to KRS 413.320 rather than an
amendment to Section 2542 of Carroll's Statutes .
Not approved .

quire the county clerk and his deputies to make such

able compensation .

WHEREAS , the Commissioner of Revenue is in agreement
with this interpretation of the Statutes involved , and ,

WHEREAS , the Commissioner of Highways in preparing
quarters for the Department of Highways and in building
streets , sidewalks and entrances to the new State Office
Building, has expended and will expend during the fiscal
years 1940-41 and 1941-42 from highway funds approxi-
mately one hundred and sixty thousand ( $ 160,000.00 ) dol-
lars , and
WHEREAS , provision was not made in the budget adopted
by the General Assembly in 1940 for the allocation of such
funds , and
WHEREAS , the General Assembly knows that such expendi-
tures were needed and necessary , NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it Resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That all expenditures of money and all funds now
obligated or to be expended by the Department of
Highways during the fiscal years 1940-1941 and
1941-1942 for partitions , equipment , streets and
sidewalks , improvements and entrance to said new
State Office Building , be and the same is now
hereby ratified and approved in a sum not to ex-
ceed one hundred and sixty thousand ( $ 160,000.00 )
dollars . Approved 11th March .

The first ratifies and approves expenditures not exceeding $160,000 made by the Department of Highways for the State Office Building during fiscal years 1940-41 and 1941-42. The second resolution authorizes R. J. In the event suit is filed and judgment rendered
by the Circuit Court , either party may appeal to
the Court of Appeals of Kentucky .
No statement in this resolution shall be considered
by either the court or a jury as an admission of
negligence upon the part of the Commonwealth or
its Department of Highways and this resolution
shall not be introduced as evidence nor commented
upon by counsel in argument to the jury .
Not approved .

classification.title: Resolution Regarding Compensation of University Personnel
classification.summary: This resolution addresses a legal dispute concerning the compensation of Presidents, Deans, Professors, and Teachers in Kentucky's educational institutions. It acknowledges that the Court of Appeals ruled that their salaries were subject to a constitutional limit of $5,000 per annum. However, due to reliance on a previous construction of the law and the inability of institutions to procure qualified staff within that limit, the General Assembly deemed it unjust to require repayment of amounts exceeding $5,000. Therefore, it resolves that the Commonwealth will not pursue claims to recover excess compensation paid to these educators.
classification.reasoning: The statute is a resolution passed by the Kentucky General Assembly in 1942. It deals with the compensation of educators in universities and colleges. The core issue is a legal interpretation of a constitutional limit on salaries and whether educators should have to repay amounts exceeding that limit. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. The statute's focus is purely on financial and legal matters related to educational personnel compensation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is from the 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY'. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates the subject matter and lack of racial component.

CHAPTER 239
(J. R. 74 )
A RESOLUTION designating June 4 as Jack Jouett Day .
WHEREAS , one of the heroes of the Revolutionary War was
Captain Jack Jouett , who saved Thomas Jefferson and the
members of the General Assembly of Virginia from cap-
ture by the British by a cross - country dash on horseback to
warn them of a secret plan to surround the members of the
General Assembly , and
WHEREAS , Captain Jouett later moved to Kentucky and
became one of the outstanding leaders of this state , and it
is right and proper that his memory should be honored ,
now therefore
Be it Resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the fourth day of June of each year be
designated and observed as Jack Jouett Day in
Kentucky , but shall not be regarded as a legal
holiday .
Not approved .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1942, p. 994

CHAPTER 248
(H. R. 9 )
WHEREAS , the poet would make the whole world a demo-
cracy , the essence of poetry being the heart of man and its
aim to proclaim the dignity of all mankind , and
WHEREAS , the poets of Kentucky have done and still do
much to elevate the thought of mankind , and
WHEREAS , some of their writings have immortalized Ken-
tucky throughout the civilized world , and
WHEREAS , the essential purpose of Kentucky Poetry Day is
to honor and give recognition to Kentucky poets , to promote
the study and appreciation of poetry , and to encourage
creative talent in this field , and
WHEREAS , Kentuckians at all times are delighted to honor
their men and women who have achieved distinction in any
calling or endeavor , now therefore ,
Be it Resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the Governor of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky is hereby authorized to designate the seventh
day of April to be set apart and to be observed an-
nually as Kentucky Poetry Day , to honor and pre-
serve the memory of the poets of Kentucky . This
day shall be observed with suitable exercises by the
citizens of the Commonwealth and by the study and
special observance of poetry in the public schools
of the State according to such program as may be
outlined by the Superintendent of Public Instruc-
tion .
Approved by the Governor , January 23 , 1942 .

for member of the General Assembly as aforesaid , and

CHAPTER 254
(H. R. 15 )
A RESOLUTION appropriating and authorizing the payment
to Joe Holcomb of the city of Jenkins , Letcher County ,
Kentucky , the sum of three hundred forty-three dollars
and twenty cents for indemnity for cattle destroyed by
order of the State Live Stock Sanitary Board .
WHEREAS , Joe Holcomb of the city of Jenkins , Letcher
County , Kentucky , was the owner of certain cattle during
the fiscal year ending June 30 , 1939 , which cattle were
destroyed by order of the State Live Stock Sanitary Board
during said fiscal year , under the provisions of section 63c- 7
of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , 1936 edition , and
WHEREAS , the State Live Stock Sanitary Board awarded
indemnity to the said Joe Holcomb to be paid by the Com-
monwealth in the sum of three hundred forty -three dollars
and twenty cents , and
WHEREAS , the said Joe Holcomb has not been paid the sum
awarded to him as indemnity because the sums appro-
priated by the General Assembly for that purpose during
that fiscal year were insufficient and sums appropriated for
subsequent fiscal years could not lawfully be expended for
that purpose , and
WHEREAS , the said Joe Holcomb has been forced to borrow
money to replace the cattle so destroyed and the lender is
demanding payment .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1942, p. 1011

CHAPTER 256
(H. R. 18 )
A JOINT RESOLUTION appropriating from the General
Fund of the Commonwealth of Kentucky forty-four dollars
and eighty cents ( $44.80 ) to pay a claim for services ren-
dered by Floyd C. Wilkey , in performance of his duties as
sheriff of Hopkins County .
WHEREAS , on November 24 , 1940 , George Allen Abernathy ,
a fugitive from justice wanted in Earlington , Kentucky , for
murder , was arrested in St. Louis , Missouri , and there
detained , by the authorities of St. Louis , Missouri , and
WHEREAS , Floyd C. Wilkey, sheriff of Hopkins County ,
traveled to St. Louis , Missouri , and conveyed George Allen
Abernathy back to Earlington , Kentucky , and
WHEREAS , Floyd C. Wilkey personally incurred expenses
totalling forty - four dollars and eighty cents ( $44.80 ) , and
WHEREAS , the claim of Floyd C. Wilkey is a just claim for
services rendered on behalf of the State , therefore
Be it Resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky ,
There is hereby appropriated from the General
Fund of the state , the sum of forty - four dollars and
eighty cents ($44.80 ) , and the Department of Fi-
nance shall draw its warrant upon the Treasurer for
that amount , in favor of Floyd C. Wilkey .
Not approved .

"ARTICLE II .
"The purpose of this compact is to conserve oil
and gas by the prevention of physical waste thereof
from any cause .
"ARTICLE III .
" Each State bound hereby agrees that within a
reasonable time it will enact laws , or if any laws
have been enacted , then it agrees to continue the
same in force , to accomplish within reasonable
limits the prevention of :
"(a) The operation of any oil well with an in-
efficient gas -oil ratio .
" "
(b) The drowning with water of any stratum
capable of producing oil or gas , or both oil and gas
in paying quantities .
"(c) The avoidable escape into the open air or
the wasteful burning of gas from a natural gas well .
" (d) The creation of unnecessary fire hazards .
"(e) The drilling , equipping , locating , spacing
or operating of a well or wells so as to bring about
physical waste of oil or gas or loss in the ultimate.
recovery thereof .
" (f) The inefficient , excessive or improper use
of the reservoir energy in producing any well .
" The enumeration of the foregoing subjects shall
not limit the scope of the authority of any State .

regulation promulgated thereunder , shall be denied

“ ARTICLE V.
"It is not the purpose of this compact to author-
ize the States joining herein to limit the production
of oil or gas for the purpose of establishing or fix-
ing the price thereof, or create or perpetuate
monopoly , or to promote regimentation , but is
limited to the purpose of conserving oil and gas and
preventing the avoidable waste thereof within
reasonable limitations .
"ARTICLE VI.
" Each State joining herein shall appoint a repre-
sentative to a commission hereby constituted and
designated as THE INTERSTATE OIL COM-
PACT COMMISSION , the duty of which commis-
sion shall be to make inquiry and ascertain from
time to time such methods , practices , circumstances
and conditions as may be disclosed for bringing
about conservation and the prevention of physical
waste of oil and gas , and at such intervals as said
commission deems beneficial it shall report its find-
ings and recommendations to the several States for
adoption or rejection .
" The commission shall have power to recom-
mend the coordination of the exercise of the police
powers of the several states within their several
jurisdictions to promote the maximum ultimate
recovery from the petroleum reserves of said
States , and to recommend measures for the maxi-
mum ultimate recovery of oil and gas . Said Com-
mission shall organize and adopt suitable rules and
regulations for the conduct of its business .

"No action shall be taken by the Commission
except : ( 1 ) by the affirmative votes of the majority
of the whole number of the compacting States ,
represented at any meeting , and ( 2 ) by a concurring
vote of a majority in interest of the compacting
States at said meeting , such interest to be deter-
mined as follows : Such vote of each State shall be
in the decimal proportion fixed by the ratio of its
daily average production during the preceding
calendar half -year to the daily average production
of the compacting States during said period .
"ARTICLE VII .
"No State by joining herein shall become
financially obligated to any other State , nor shall
the breach of the terms hereof by any State subject
such State to financial responsibility to the other
States joining herein .

"This compact shall expire September 1 , 1937 .
But any State joining herein may , upon sixty ( 60 )
days ' notice , withdraw herefrom.

CHAPTER 278 989
CHAPTER 277
(H. R. 41 )
A JOINT RESOLUTION appropriating to T. R. Sullivan , for-
mer Deputy Sheriff of Russell County the sum of $ 72.00 for
expenses incurred in connection with acting as agent on
the part of the state for the return of a fugitive from
justice .
WHEREAS , T. R. Sullivan , former Deputy Sheriff of Russell
County was on June 4, 1938 made the agent of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky to go to the State of North Caro-
lina for the purpose of demanding and receiving from the
proper authorities of that state one Alva Morgan , a fugitive
from justice from the State of Kentucky , and
WHEREAS , the said T. R. Sullivan went to the State of North
Carolina for the purpose of returning the fugitive , and
WHEREAS , the Governor of the State of North Carolina
refused to honor the requisition for the return of said
fugitive , and
WHEREAS , the expenses incurred by T. R. Sullivan in going
to and returning from the State of North Carolina as agent
for the Commonwealth amounted to the sum of $ 72.00 , and
said expenses have not been paid to the said T. R. Sullivan
because of the refusal of the Governor of the State of
North Carolina to honor the requisition of the Governor of
this Commonwealth , NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it Resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That there is hereby appropriated out of the
general fund to T. R. Sullivan the sum of $72.00 and
the Department of Finance is hereby directed to
draw its warrant upon the Treasurer in favor of
T. R. Sullivan for said amount .
Not approved .

The suit is limited to a maximum of two thousand dollars. The Attorney General is empowered to investigate and settle the claim. Any recovered amount will be paid from the General Fund. Either party may appeal a Circuit Court judgment to the Court of Appeals. The resolution explicitly states it is not an admission of negligence and cannot be used as evidence.

to sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky in the

the Court of Appeals of Kentucky .

sidered by either the jury or court as an admission

and this resolution shall not be introduced as evi-
dence , nor commented upon by counsel in the argu-
ment to the jury .
Not approved .

CHAPTER 293 .
(H. R. 62 )
JOINT RESOLUTION appropriating from the General Fund
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , $ 69.02 , to pay a claim
for services rendered by W. O. Mitchell in performance of
his duties as sheriff of Caldwell County ;
WHEREAS , Stanley McCarty , a fugitive from justice , wanted
in Princeton , Kentucky , for child desertion , was arrested
on July 10 , 1941 , and detained by the authorities of Detroit ,
Michigan ; and
WHEREAS , W. O. Mitchell in the performance of his duties
as sheriff of Caldwell County , on his own expense , per-
sonally traveled to Detroit , Michigan , and conveyed Stanley
McCarty of Princeton , Kentucky ; and
WHEREAS , W. O. Mitchell personally incurred expenses
totaling $ 69.02 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
The sum of $ 69.02 is hereby appropriated from
the General Fund of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky , and the Department of Finance shall draw
its warrant for the amount upon the State Treasurer
in favor of W. O. Mitchell .
Not approved .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1942, p. 1056

CHAPTER 294 .
(H. R. 63 )
RESOLUTION for the benefit of Doyle Hogg ex - sheriff of
Letcher County , Kentucky , to reimburse him for expenses
incurred and services rendered the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky in the year 1940 in going from Whitesburg , Ken-
tucky to Spartansburg , South Carolina to return to Letcher
County Kentucky , one Charlie Stacy , a fugitive from
justice .
WHEREAS , in the month of December , 1940 , one Charlie
Stacy , a fugitive from justice , and who stood indicted in
Letcher County , Kentucky upon the charges of murder and
rape , was arrested in Spartanburg County , South Caro-
lina , and was being held by the Sheriff of said county for
the authorities of Letcher County , Kentucky , upon said
charges, and Whereas , the said Charlie Stacy waived extra-
dition from South Carolina to the State of Kentucky upon
said charges , and whereas , Doyle Hogg , Sheriff of Letcher
County went from Whitesburg , Kentucky to Spartanburg ,

Foundation for the rearing and protection of wild animals
and wild turkeys of all kind , and whereas the Common-
wealth has , through its Fish and Game Commission , placed
on said land large numbers of deer and same have been
prolific and increased to a very large number , and are not
confined on the above said land , but are permitted to run
in large droves over land adoining and surrounding this
said 15,000 acres and destroy the crops of the farmers in
the surrounding country , and said deer are by sections
1938a - 16 and 1938a - 17 and amendments thereto of Carroll's
Kentucky Statutes , Baldwin's Revision , protected from
being killed or chased by dogs .
WHEREAS , Richard Cox and W. F. Masden are farmers in
the surrounding territory of the above said 15,000 acres of
land and were such farmers during the years 1937 , 1938 ,
1939 , 1940 and 1941 and during the above said years the
deer as above said have gone upon the land cultivated by
Richard Cox and destroyed his crops and damaged him in
the sum of $ 3,360.00 and have gone upon the land culti-
vated by W. F. Masden during said years and destroyed
his crops and damaged him in the sum of $ 2,100.00 .
NOW, in order that Richard Cox and W. F. Masden and each
of them may have their " Day in Court " to determine by
judicial action the extent of damages , if any , sustained by
them , and the liability therefor .

Commonwealth of Kentucky :

each of them , in their own right and name , be and
they are respectively and severally authorized to
sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky , and the State

CHAPTER 4
( S. Res. 10. )
A JOINT RESOLUTION appropriating money for the 1942
Special Session of the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , and declaring an emergency to exist .
Be it Resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. There is hereby appropriated out of
the General Expenditure Fund for the payment of
salaries and other necessary expenses of the 1942
Special Session of the General Assembly the sum of
Fifty Thousand ( $ 50,000 ) Dollars .
Section 2. Whereas , the funds heretofore ap-
propriated for the 1942 General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky are exhausted , and
Whereas , the work of the 1942 Special Session
of the General Assembly is not completed , an emer-
gency is declared to exist and this resolution shall
become effective immediately upon its passage and
aproval by the Governor .
Approved April 2 , 1942 .

classification.title: ROADS AND HIGHWAYS - RURAL ELECTRIC COOPERATIVE CORPORATIONS - SABOTAGE PREVENTION
classification.summary: This document contains index entries and partial statute text related to Roads and Highways, Rural Electric Cooperative Corporations, and Sabotage Prevention. The Roads and Highways section details plans, specifications, expenses, public road districts, state highway primary systems, and routes. The Rural Electric Cooperative Corporations section covers bonds and debt obligations, including authority to issue, interim receipts, issuance for money or property, price, redemption, resolutions, sale, and security agreements.
